meta.name: Yi Shiu Pty Ltd v Brisbane City Council & Anor; Burrell & Ors v Brisbane City Council & Anor

one.zh: 相邻业主(Yi Shiu、Burrell)就 Council 批准 Dibcorp 在 Spring Hill 一处场地建 17 层、含 119 套住宅的混合用途开发提出提交人上诉。法院认定该开发实质符合规划方案,仅三处非合规(货车通道设计、缺深层种植、层数与社区预期不符)均属技术性、无不利规划后果;Dibcorp 已尽举证责任,上诉将在拟妥修订条件后部分获准、开发获批。本次命令为将上诉排入申请名录复核(2026-05-06),以拟备修订条件。
one.en: Adjoining owners (Yi Shiu and Burrell) brought submitter appeals against Council's approval of Dibcorp's 17-storey, 119-unit mixed-use development on a Spring Hill site. The Court found the development substantially complied with the planning scheme; the only three non-compliances (the service-vehicle access design, absence of deep planting, and storey numbers not aligned to community expectations) were technical and sounded in no adverse planning consequences. Dibcorp discharged the onus; the appeals will in due course be allowed in part and the application approved. The order made today adjourns the appeals to the applications list for review on 6 May 2026 to settle amended conditions.

issue.zh: 在开发部分不符合 Council 规划方案(尤其是 Spring Hill 邻里规划规范 SHNPC)的情况下,是否仍应批准该开发?——核心争点包括:开发是否为场地所「过度体量」;通往 Little Edward Street 的货车通道设计是否不可接受。
issue.en: Should the development be approved in the face of non-compliance with Council's planning scheme (in particular the Spring Hill neighbourhood plan code, SHNPC)? Central issues include whether the development is over-scaled for the site, and whether the design of the service-vehicle access to Little Edward Street is unacceptable.

subseq.zh: 上诉排入申请名录,于 2026-05-06 上午 9:15 复核,以促成拟备一套修订后的批准条件;在就条件产生争议前,上诉将留在该名录。法院表明:上诉终将部分获准,开发申请在修订条件下获批。
subseq.en: The appeals were adjourned to the applications list for review at 9:15am on 6 May 2026 to facilitate an amended suite of conditions; they remain on that list unless and until a dispute about conditions arises. The Court indicated the appeals will in due course be allowed in part, with the application approved subject to amended conditions.

summary.catchwords: PLANNING AND ENVIRONMENT – APPEAL – where the co-respondent seeks approval for a mixed use development comprising a 119 unit Multiple dwelling and Centre activities – where the development application was approved by the respondent subject to conditions – where the proposed development complies, in part, with the respondent's planning scheme – where the non-compliance does not sound in adverse planning consequences – whether an approval should be granted in the face of non-compliance with the respondent's planning scheme.

hint.en: The whole case as a story

html.zh:
Dibcorp（共同答辩人）欲在 Spring Hill 一处 1,578 平方米的场地重建——位于 Leichhardt Street 与 Little Edward Street 交界西北角。拟建为 17 层塔楼加裙楼形式：塔楼含 119 套1、2、3 房住宅；裙楼 2 至 3 层，含一系列 Centre activities，并整合、修复了地标性的原 Spring Hill Hotel 立面。

Dibcorp 向 Council 提出影响评估开发申请。经公开通知收到 6 份提交（支持与反对参半），Council 授权代表附条件批准了该申请（2024-09-27 决定通知）。

两名相邻业主提起提交人上诉：Yi Shiu（场地以西 88 Leichhardt Street 有权益）与 Burrell（场地以北 24 Little Edward Street 有权益）。上诉主要围绕 Spring Hill 邻里规划规范（SHNPC）展开，争点包括：塔楼是否够「纤细（slender）」、是否体现「亚热带建筑」；开发是否「过度体量（over-scaled）」；是否「从属于（subordinate）」City Centre expansion precinct；以及（Burrell 上诉）通往 Little Edward Street 的货车通道设计是否不可接受。

法院逐一处理争点后认定：开发实质符合 SHNPC，仅三处非合规——货车通道设计、缺深层种植、层数与社区预期不符——且均属技术性、不产生不利规划后果。Dibcorp 已尽举证责任，开发申请终将在修订条件下获批。
html.en:
Dibcorp (the co-respondent) wished to redevelop a 1,578m² site in Spring Hill, on the north-western edge of the Leichhardt Street / Little Edward Street intersection. The proposal is a 17-storey tower-and-podium form: the tower contains 119 residential units (1, 2 and 3 bedroom); the 2-to-3-storey podium contains a mix of Centre activities and integrates the refurbished façade of the former, landmark Spring Hill Hotel.

Dibcorp made an impact assessable development application to Council. After public notification attracting six submissions (a mix of support and opposition), Council's delegate approved it subject to conditions (decision notice 27 September 2024).

Two adjoining owners brought submitter appeals: Yi Shiu (an interest in land to the west, 88 Leichhardt Street) and Burrell (an interest to the north, 24 Little Edward Street). The appeals centred on the Spring Hill neighbourhood plan code (SHNPC), with issues including whether the tower was sufficiently slender and exhibited subtropical architecture; whether the development was over-scaled; whether it was subordinate to the City Centre expansion precinct; and (in the Burrell appeal) whether the service-vehicle access to Little Edward Street was unacceptable.

Working through each issue, the Court found the development substantially complied with the SHNPC, with only three non-compliances — the service-vehicle access design, the absence of deep planting, and storey numbers not aligned to community expectations — all of which were technical and sounded in no adverse planning consequences. Dibcorp discharged the onus and the application will in due course be approved subject to amended conditions.

html.zh:
性质：昆士兰规划环境法院（QPEC）中的两宗提交人上诉（Yi Shiu 与 Burrell），针对 Council 授权代表附条件批准 Dibcorp 开发申请的决定；由 Williamson KC DCJ 于 2026-04-24 判决，聆讯历时七日（2026 年 2 月）。

评估框架：上诉按重新聆讯（hearing anew）进行（P&E Court Act 2016 ss 43、46(1)）；法院立于评估管理者之位，依《规划法 2016》s 45(5) 评估、依 s 59(3) 与 s 60 决定；须顾及规章所定事项，含 s 31(1)(g) 的共同材料（含 6 份提交）（经 P&E Court Act 2016 s 46(2)(a)）。举证责任由 Dibcorp 承担（P&E Court Act 2016 s 45）。

争点与裁断：法院逐一处理 SHNPC 各整体成果（OO 3f、3g、3i、6b）、绩效成果（PO1、PO3），以及「是否过度体量」「货车通道是否不可接受」两大综合争点，均作出有利于批准的认定。

今日命令（处分）：将上诉排入申请名录，于 2026-05-06 上午 9:15 复核，以拟备一套修订条件。法院表明：上诉终将部分获准，开发申请在修订条件下获批。
html.en:
Nature: two submitter appeals (Yi Shiu and Burrell) in the Planning and Environment Court of Queensland (QPEC) against Council's delegate's conditional approval of Dibcorp's application; decided by Williamson KC DCJ on 24 April 2026 after a seven-day hearing (February 2026).

Assessment framework: the appeal proceeds by way of hearing anew (ss 43 and 46(1) of the P&E Court Act 2016); the Court stands in the assessment manager's position, assessing under s 45(5) of the Planning Act 2016 and deciding under s 59(3) and s 60; it must have regard to prescribed matters including the common material under s 31(1)(g) (the six submissions) (via s 46(2)(a) of the P&E Court Act 2016). The onus lies on Dibcorp (s 45 of the P&E Court Act 2016).

Issues and findings: the Court worked through the SHNPC overall outcomes (OO 3f, 3g, 3i, 6b) and performance outcomes (PO1, PO3), and the two composite issues of whether the development was over-scaled and whether the service-vehicle access was unacceptable, resolving each in favour of approval.

Order made today (disposition): the appeals were adjourned to the applications list for review at 9:15am on 6 May 2026 to settle an amended suite of conditions. The Court indicated the appeals will in due course be allowed in part, with the application approved subject to amended conditions.

html.zh:
§ s45 ›
§ s59 ›
§ s60 ›
§ s31 ›
§ s43 ›
§ s45PECA ›
§ s46 ›
框架
评估决定框架由《规划法 2016》与《规划环境法院法 2016》共同构成：影响评估申请依 s 45(5) 评估、依 s 59(3) 与 s 60 决定；须顾及《规划规章 2017》s 31 所定共同材料（含提交）。程序上,上诉按重新聆讯进行(PECA ss 43、46(1)),举证责任在开发方(PECA s 45),并经 PECA s 46(2)(a) 顾及规章所定事项。
⚖ 依 Abeleda、YQ Property、Wilhelm、Trinity Park 等上诉权威,s 60(3) 所赋裁量较其法定前身更具弹性,允许在开发不符合规划方案时仍予批准;同时,规划方案仍是公共利益的反映。
html.en:
§ s45 ›
§ s59 ›
§ s60 ›
§ s31 ›
§ s43 ›
§ s45PECA ›
§ s46 ›
Framework
The assessment and decision framework is set by the Planning Act 2016 and the Planning and Environment Court Act 2016: an impact assessable application is assessed under s 45(5) and decided under s 59(3) and s 60; regard must be had to the common material prescribed by s 31 of the Planning Regulation 2017 (including submissions). Procedurally, the appeal proceeds by hearing anew (PECA ss 43, 46(1)), the onus lies on the development side (PECA s 45), and prescribed matters are had regard to via PECA s 46(2)(a).
⚖ Per the Court of Appeal authorities (Abeleda, YQ Property, Wilhelm, Trinity Park), the s 60(3) discretion is more flexible than its predecessor, permitting approval despite non-compliance with a planning scheme; a planning scheme nonetheless remains a reflection of the public interest.

html.zh:
本案援引以下判例（判决在「CASES」中列明）：

Abeleda v Brisbane City Council、Brisbane City Council v YQ Property、Wilhelm v Logan City Council、Trinity Park Investments v Cairns Regional Council; Dexus v Fabcot——四则昆士兰上诉法院权威,确立《规划法 2016》s 60(3) 评估决定框架的弹性进路：非合规不等于必然拒绝,规划方案仍反映公共利益。

Heilbronn & Partners v Brisbane City Council——就涉及公共安全的事项应采较保守进路(用于评估货车通道安全性)。Peach v Brisbane City Council——就规划方案解释与评估进路提供辅助支持。
html.en:
This case cites the following authorities (as listed under "CASES"):

Abeleda v Brisbane City Council, Brisbane City Council v YQ Property, Wilhelm v Logan City Council, and Trinity Park Investments v Cairns Regional Council; Dexus v Fabcot — four Queensland Court of Appeal authorities establishing the flexible approach to the s 60(3) assessment/decision framework: non-compliance does not mean automatic refusal, and a planning scheme remains a reflection of the public interest.

Heilbronn & Partners v Brisbane City Council — a conservative approach is called for on matters of public safety (applied to the service-vehicle access). Peach v Brisbane City Council — supporting guidance on planning-scheme interpretation and the approach to assessment.

html.zh:
1. 评估进路 —— 依上诉权威（Abeleda 等），s 60(3) 裁量具弹性，允许在非合规下批准；但规划方案仍反映公共利益。SHNPC 提供最本地化的规划，对其评估具重大乃至决定性分量。

2. 「纤细塔楼」与「亚热带建筑」（OO 3i、3g） —— 法院以「普通含义」解释这两个未定义用语。就 3i，重点不在塔楼三维比例的视觉印象，而在其是否经选址与设计以「维持街景开阔、提供充足楼间距」这一规划目的。法院认定该规划目的已达成，合规成立；纵有非合规，亦属技术性、无不利后果。就 3g，证据充分显示设计已寻求减少对机械供暖、制冷、照明的依赖，达成亚热带气候回应。

3. 「从属性」（OO 6b） —— 6b 要求开发在高度与规模上「从属于」City Centre expansion precinct。法院接受这属事实、程度与印象问题，并借 Table 7.2.19.5.3.B 所示的预期高度差与地形等既有特征评估，认定该开发确属从属，合规成立。

4. 绩效成果 PO1、PO3 —— 就 PO1，法院认定已部分合规；与 PO1e（层数与社区预期一致）不符者属技术性、无不利规划后果。就 PO3，法院认定合规——上诉方所指诸般不利影响不为其所接受的证据、亦不为照片蒙太奇所支持。

5. 是否「过度体量」 —— 上诉方以一组指标（对街景与相邻地的影响、俯视、依赖相邻地提供的间距等）主张开发过度体量。法院不接受这些指标在本案中成立，故不认为该开发对场地而言过大。

6. 货车通道是否不可接受 —— 法院采较保守进路（Heilbronn）评估公共安全，认定所提通道能为出场货车提供充足视距、为行人／单车／电动滑板车提供可接受的安全水平。虽存在与 TAPS 规范 PO1b／TAPS 政策的非合规，但因不产生不利交通安全后果，不应赋予重大分量。

7. 裁量与结论 —— 开发实质符合规划方案，仅三处非合规（货车通道、缺深层种植、层数与社区预期不符），均属技术性、无不利规划后果。综合其整体优点与实质合规，强烈支持批准；须有令人信服的规划理由方能拒绝，而此等理由并不存在。Dibcorp 已尽举证责任；上诉终将部分获准、开发申请在修订条件下获批。为拟备修订条件，法院将上诉排入申请名录于 2026-05-06 复核。
html.en:
1. Approach to assessment — per the Court of Appeal authorities (Abeleda etc.), the s 60(3) discretion is flexible and permits approval despite non-compliance; but a planning scheme still reflects the public interest. The SHNPC provides the most locally focused planning and attracts significant, potentially decisive, weight.

2. 'Slender towers' and 'subtropical architecture' (OO 3i, 3g) — the Court gave both undefined phrases their ordinary meaning. For 3i, the focus is not the visual impression of the tower's three-dimensional proportions but whether it is sited and designed to serve the planning purpose of maintaining open street vistas with adequate separation. The Court found that purpose met, so compliance was made out; any non-compliance was technical and sounded in no adverse consequence. For 3g, the evidence comfortably showed the design sought to minimise the need for mechanical heating, cooling and lighting, achieving a subtropical response.

3. 'Subordinate' (OO 6b) — 6b requires development to be subordinate in height and scale to the City Centre expansion precinct. The Court accepted this is a matter of fact, degree and impression, and, assisted by the expected height differential in Table 7.2.19.5.3.B and existing features such as topography, found the development was subordinate; compliance was made out.

4. Performance outcomes PO1 and PO3 — for PO1, the Court found partial compliance; the non-compliance with PO1e (storeys aligned to community expectations) was technical and sounded in no adverse planning consequence. For PO3, the Court found compliance — the adverse impacts alleged by the appellants were not supported by the evidence the Court accepted, nor by the photomontages.

5. Whether 'over-scaled' — the appellants relied on a set of indicia (impacts on the streetscape and adjoining land, overlooking, reliance on separation provided by adjoining land, etc.) to say the development was over-scaled. The Court did not accept those indicia were present and so did not accept the development was too big for the site.

6. Whether the service-vehicle access was unacceptable — taking a conservative approach on public safety (Heilbronn), the Court found the proposed access gave sufficient visibility for exiting service vehicles and an acceptable level of safety for pedestrians, cyclists and e-scooters. Although there was non-compliance with PO1b of the TAPS code / the TAPS policy, it did not sound in adverse traffic-safety consequences and should be given little weight.

7. Discretion and conclusion — the development substantially complied, with only three non-compliances (service-vehicle access, absence of deep planting, and storeys not aligned to community expectations), all technical and sounding in no adverse planning consequence. Its overall merit and substantial compliance strongly favoured approval; compelling planning reasons would be needed to refuse, and none existed. Dibcorp discharged the onus; the appeals will in due course be allowed in part and the application approved subject to amended conditions. To settle those conditions, the Court adjourned the appeals to the applications list for review on 6 May 2026.

html.zh:
裁判要旨

依《规划法 2016》s 60(3)（经 Abeleda、YQ Property、Wilhelm、Trinity Park 阐释），开发与规划方案的非合规并不必然导致拒绝：法院可整体权衡，在实质合规且非合规不产生不利规划后果时批准开发。规划方案中未定义的用语（如「纤细塔楼」「亚热带建筑」）依其普通含义解释，且重在其所服务的规划目的而非纯视觉印象。

「从属性」等定性标准属事实、程度与印象问题；涉公共安全的争点（如车辆通道）须采较保守进路（Heilbronn），但若非合规不产生不利安全后果，则分量有限。举证责任在开发方（PECA s 45）；本案 Dibcorp 已尽责任，开发终将在修订条件下获批。
html.en:
Ratio

Under s 60(3) of the Planning Act 2016 (as explained by Abeleda, YQ Property, Wilhelm and Trinity Park), non-compliance with a planning scheme does not automatically require refusal: the Court may weigh the matter holistically and approve where there is substantial compliance and the non-compliance sounds in no adverse planning consequence. Undefined terms in a planning scheme (such as 'slender towers' and 'subtropical architecture') are given their ordinary meaning, with focus on the planning purpose they serve rather than mere visual impression.

Qualitative standards such as being 'subordinate' are questions of fact, degree and impression; issues going to public safety (such as vehicle access) call for a more conservative approach (Heilbronn), but carry limited weight if the non-compliance produces no adverse safety consequence. The onus is on the development side (PECA s 45); here Dibcorp discharged it, and the development will in due course be approved subject to amended conditions.

html.zh:
Submitter appeal · 提交人上诉
对开发申请提出过提交的人(常为相邻业主)对批准决定提起的上诉。
Impact assessable · 影响评估
须公开通知、可接受提交的较高档评估;评估范围较广。
Assessment benchmark · 评估基准
评估开发申请所依据的规划方案标准;须为申请妥为提出时有效者。
SHNPC · Spring Hill 邻里规划规范
针对 Spring Hill 的「更细颗粒度」本地规划,含整体成果与绩效／可接受成果。
Overall / Performance outcome · 整体成果／绩效成果
规划方案中表述规划目标(整体成果)与达成方式(绩效成果)的条文。
Subordinate · 从属
开发在高度与规模上须次于另一区(此处为 City Centre expansion precinct);属事实与程度问题。
Slender tower · 纤细塔楼
高度相对横向尺度显著偏大的塔楼形态;本案依其普通含义与所服务规划目的评估。
Deep planting · 深层种植
种植于自然地面之上、可培育大型遮荫树的种植方式;本案缺此,但被认定非合规属技术性。
Onus · 举证责任
在 QPEC 上诉中须说服法院的一方;本案由开发方(Dibcorp)承担。
html.en:
Submitter appeal
An appeal against an approval by a person who made a submission on the application (often an adjoining owner).
Impact assessable
A higher level of assessment requiring public notification and allowing submissions; broader in scope.
Assessment benchmark
The planning-scheme standard against which an application is assessed; the one in effect when the application was properly made.
SHNPC
The Spring Hill neighbourhood plan code — finer-grained local planning for Spring Hill, with overall outcomes and performance/acceptable outcomes.
Overall / Performance outcome
Planning-scheme provisions stating the planning objectives (overall outcomes) and how they are achieved (performance outcomes).
Subordinate
Development required to be lesser in height and scale than another area (here the City Centre expansion precinct); a matter of fact and degree.
Slender tower
A tower form whose height is markedly greater in proportion to its lateral dimensions; assessed here by its ordinary meaning and the planning purpose it serves.
Deep planting
Planting located over natural ground able to sustain large shade trees; absent here, but the non-compliance was found to be technical.
Onus
The party who must persuade the Court in a QPEC appeal; here borne by the development side (Dibcorp).

html.zh:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

WILLIAMSON KC DCJ:

Introduction

[1]The co-respondent (Dibcorp) wishes to redevelop a 1,578m 2 site on the north-western edge of the intersection of Leichhardt Street and Little Edward Street, Spring Hill (the site). The proposed redevelopment is a 17 storey high rise with a tower and podium form. The tower contains 119 residential units with a mix of 1, 2 and 3 bedroom configurations. The podium is made up of ‘new’ and ‘old’ building fabric. The new component is 2 to 3 storeys in height, built to boundary and contains a mix of Centre activities. The old fabric, which is integrated with the new, comprises the refurbished façade, and part of the interior, of the former Spring Hill Hotel. The hotel is visually prominent at the corner of Leichhardt Street and Little Edward Street.

[2]To facilitate the redevelopment of the site, Dibcorp made an impact assessable development application to Council, seeking a number of development permits. The application was publicly notified and attracted six submissions (Ex.49). The response to the development by submitters was a mix of support and opposition. After completing the assessment under the Planning Act 2016 (PA), Council’s delegate decided to approve Dibcorp’s development application. A conditional development approval was granted. The approval was communicated by way of decision notice dated 27 September 2024 (Ex.45).

[3]There are two proceedings before the Court. They are submitter appeals against the delegate’s decision to grant a conditional development approval. The submitter appellants are adjoining landowners. Yi Shiu Pty Ltd (Yi Shiu) has an interest in adjoining land to the west of the site at 88 Leichhardt Street. The collection of appellants to the Burrell appeal (Burrell) have an interest in adjoining land to the north of the site at 24 Little Edward Street. Yi Shiu and Burrell contend for refusal.

[4]The submitter appeals were heard together and proceeded by way of hearing anew: ss 43 and 46(1), Planning and Environment Court Act 2016.

[5]The issue in dispute between the parties is broadly stated as whether Dibcorp’s development application should be approved or refused. Save for a traffic engineering issue raised only in the Burrell appeal, the submitter appellants advanced a joint case for refusal. The refusal case is founded on three key assertions (Ex.29, para 5; Ex.63, para 3), namely that: (1) the proposed redevelopment is over-scaled for the site, resulting in various impacts and non-compliances with the adopted planning controls; (2) the proposed development is of a height and scale that will not be subordinate to an area designated ‘City Centre expansion precinct’, resulting in non-compliances with the adopted planning controls; and (3) relevant matters advanced by Dibcorp do not support approval in the face of adverse impacts and non-compliances with the adopted planning controls.

[6]The Spring Hill neighbourhood plan code (the SHNPC), which forms part of Council’s planning scheme, has a significant role to play in the assessment of the development application and the exercise of the planning discretion. The appeals were argued on the footing that the neighbourhood plan represents the most specific planning for the land, and the outcome of an assessment against it weighs very heavily in, if not determinative of, the exercise of the planning discretion: Ex.61, para 8. This joint approach to the question of approval/refusal in the two appeals is the correct one.

[7]Dibcorp and Council resist the submitter appeals.

[8]It is for Dibcorp to discharge the onus: s 45, Planning and Environment Court Act 2016. The development site and locality

[9]Spring Hill is one of the oldest suburbs of Brisbane, adjoining the northern edge of the central business district (CBD). The site is centrally located within Spring Hill.

[10]Planning scheme maps, read with aerial photography, reveal Spring Hill has a limited number of major roads providing an east-west or north-south access into, and through, the suburb. For present purposes, three prominent roads are of note; Boundary Street, Leichhardt Street and Little Edward Street. Boundary Street is located one block to the north of the site and provides a direct east-west connection through Spring Hill. Leichhardt Street is located to the south of the site. It meanders along a ridgeline, providing a northeast-west connection through Spring Hill. Little Edward Street is located to the east of the site and forms part of a north-south connection, linking Leichhardt Street and Boundary Street. The site is situated on the north-western corner of the intersection of Leichhardt Street and Little Edward Street, which is heavily trafficked.

[11]The existing land use and built form character of Spring Hill varies considerably. Working north to south through the suburb, the area north and north-east of Boundary Street is predominantly traditional timber and tin character housing. An existing District centre is located to the south of the character housing area, sleeving Boundary Street between Douglas Street and Bradley Street. Existing land uses south of Boundary Street and north of the CBD comprise a broad mix of commercial, institutional, residential and short-term accommodation uses. The associated built form varies considerably in terms of height, scale, age and architectural design.

[12]The most intense form of development is located south of Leichhardt Street and north of the CBD. Building heights in this area range from 4 to 27 storeys. This can be contrasted with building heights, which range from 1 to 10 storeys, north of Leichhardt Street and south of Boundary Street. The disparity in building height, taken in combination with the undulating nature of local topography, particularly from Leichhardt Street south to the CBD, results in a fragmented skyline of built form.

[13]The general ‘feel’ of Spring Hill was helpfully described by Mr Ovenden in this way, which I accept (Ex.8, p.9): “…the locality can be described as [a] (sic) dynamic urban area characterised by a blend of old and new. It features a mix of character buildings, low rise dwellings, and mid to high rise commercial, educational and residential buildings. Both Leichhardt Street and Little Edward Street are key vehicular and pedestrian spines that run through Spring Hill with Leichhardt Street providing a main thoroughfare to the CBD and Little Edward Street connecting Leichhardt Street with Boundary Street. The area’s proximity to major transport hubs and the Brisbane CBD positions it as a key area for future growth and investment, led by the Spring Hill neighbourhood plan.”

[14]Turning to the site, it is located within the block framed by Leichhardt Street/Little Edward Street/Boundary Street/Fortescue Street. The site (Ex.8, p.8): (a) comprises two lots with a combined area of 1,578m 2; (b) is trapezoidal in shape; (c) has a 35.29 metre frontage to Leichhardt Street (to the south); (d) has a 51.56 metre frontage to Little Edward Street (to the east); (e) is not burdened or benefitted with any easements; and (f) is improved with the remnants of the façade of a two-storey local heritage building (the former Spring Hill Hotel), built to boundary at the corner of Leichhardt Street and Little Edward Street.

[15]The site’s northern boundary is adjoined by a dated 6 storey brick faced commercial building. The property is identified as 24 Little Edward Street (i.e. the Burrell land). This is a small commercial site, having an area of 440m 2 and a frontage width of 13 metres. The brick building has a 3 metre set back to Little Edward Street; is built to the common boundary with the site; and has a 2 metre setback to the north. The setback to the north is part of a shared driveway with land to the north.

[16]The site’s western boundary is adjoined by a 3 storey commercial building and commercial carpark. The adjoining property is identified as 88 Leichhardt Street (i.e. the Yi Shiu land). It has an area of 2,568m 2 and frontages of 55 metres to Fortescue Street and 40 metres to Leichhardt Street. The commercial building is of contemporary design and appearance, with a setback of 6 metres to Fortescue Street and about 16 metres to Leichhardt Street. The latter setback is fenced and landscaped. It provides an external recreation area for students who attend the trade and industry college operated from the premises.

[17]The three remaining corners of the intersection of Leichhardt Street and Little Edward Street are improved with the following built form: (a) a 4 storey hotel in the north-eastern corner; (b) a 5 to 6 storey office building in the south-eastern corner; and (c) a 12 storey office building in the south-western corner.

[18]Development approvals have been granted by Council that, if acted upon, facilitate intense redevelopment of land in Spring Hill. Two of the approvals are for land located within the block identified at

[14]. A third approval is for land located south-east of the site on Leichhardt Street.

[19]The first approval is an amalgamated parcel (2,691m 2) located only a few lots to the north of the site; it was identified in the evidence as 25 Fortescue Street. The amalgamated parcel has two frontages; one to Fortescue Street and one to Little Edward Street: Ex.16, para 31. Approvals were granted by Council in 2020 (Ex.51) and 2024 (Ex.52) for this land. Both approvals envisage two high rise towers (15 and 14/15 storeys) offset in ‘plan’ view, sitting above a multi-storey basement carpark. The approvals also provide for a cross-block connection between Little Edward Street and Fortescue Street. The height of the 2020 development (excluding roof elements) is RL 96.30. The height of the 2024 development (excluding roof elements) is RL 92.80. This can be compared to the proposed development, which has a height (excluding roof elements) of RL 91.20. Contrary to the appellants’ submissions, the proposed development would not, if approved, be the tallest building approved by Council in the block identified at

[14]. Nor would it be the tallest building in the ‘Local Area’ defined in the Visual amenity and landscape architecture joint report.

[20]The form of development authorised by the 2020 approval is depicted, by way of ghosting, in the photomontages prepared for this appeal. The ghosted built form is: (1) bulky; (2) appreciably greater in scale, and height, in comparison to existing built form in the block identified at

[14]; and (3) is not fairly described as a slender tower form. The same observations apply to the form of development authorised by the 2024 approval. A review of the photomontages does not suggest Dibcorp’s proposed development, by reason of its height and scale, will dominate or be out of character with the approved development at 25 Fortescue Street.

[21]The second approval attaches to a 410m 2 site located at 36 Little Edward Street, again, only a few lots to the north of the site (Ex.53). The development approval attaching to this land authorises a 9 storey development (6 storey tower above a 3 storey podium). The podium is built to boundary on Little Edward Street. The tower depicted in the approved plans appears slender in its presentation to Little Edward Street. The approved form of development is depicted, by way of ghosting, in the photomontages. A review of the photomontages does not suggest Dibcorp’s proposed development, by reason of its height or scale, will be out of character or dwarf the approved development at 36 Little Edward Street.

[22]A third approval was given for land located to the south-east of the site at 157 Leichhardt Street (Ex.54). The development approval was granted by Council in February 2023. It authorises a mixed use redevelopment (108 units and Centre activities) in a tower and podium form. The tower is slender in its presentation to Leichhardt Street. It is in the order of 23 storeys high (plus a lift over run and open space at roof level). The approved form of development is depicted, by way of ghosting, in the photomontages. The photomontages confirm the proposed development will be subordinate to the height, bulk and scale of the development authorised by the approval for 157 Leichhardt Street. The proposed development

[23]The development application before the Court is impact assessable and seeks, inter alia, a development permit to start a new use of the site. Three defined uses are proposed; Multiple dwelling (119 units), Food and drink outlet and Shop. The non- residential uses form part of the Centre activities ‘activity group’ in the planning scheme. None of the refusal issues go to the land uses proposed. The issues are directed to the height, bulk and scale of built form.

[24]The form of the proposed development is depicted in a series of architectural (Ex.4) and landscape (Ex.5) plans and photomontages (Exs.6 and 23). To complete the development picture, the visual aids need to be read in conjunction with a bundle of conditions Dibcorp ‘would be willing to accept’ as part of an approval (Ex.7, Tabs 21 and 21a).

[25]The architectural plans, and photomontages, depict a 17 storey mixed use building with a tower, podium and basement carparking.

[26]The podium comprises two complementary elements that combine to create an integrated base of the building. The first element is the partial refurbishment of the Spring Hill Hotel. The façade is to be painted white. It is two storeys in height and built to boundary, wrapping around the corner of Leichhardt Street and Little Edward Street. The second element comprises two separate podium façades which, save for an opening for vehicular access and a service bay, are built to boundary on Leichhardt Street and Little Edward Street. They provide an active ‘green edge’. The new elements of the podium are visually separated by the heritage façade. The heritage façade punctuates the new podium at the south-east corner. This is achieved by a combination of: (1) the podium cantilevering over the hotel; (2) the use of different colours; and (3) the provision of a deep multi-storey recess in the podium. The deep recess has an interesting visual effect; it creates a visual relationship whereby the new components of the podium and tower above defer to, and are distinguishable from, the heritage façade. A strong visual delineation between the heritage hotel and the new podium has been achieved in the design. It is a feature, in my view, that will make an attractive contribution to the streetscape of this part of Spring Hill.

[27]Dr Robinson is an architect well known to the Court. He carefully examined the design of the proposed development and described the new components of the podium in this way: “49. The new two storey podium form fronting Leichhardt Street is approximately 12m in width and accommodates a double height retail space, behind a landscaped colonnade at ground level…That colonnade continues up through a full frontage width planter at level 1. The podium element’s parapet, which has a height alignment just lower than the parapet of the heritage hotel building is similarly lined with a landscape planter box. The planter box both conceals and provides an edge to a podium top residential courtyard. 50. The new podium form fronting Little Edward Street is approximately 30.4m in width and has a height of two storeys where adjacent to the two storey hotel building which, due to the fall in the natural ground line increases to three storeys in height at the interface with the Burrell Site to the north…The basement level, which has direct access and frontage to Little Edward Street, accommodates retail space, a well camouflaged and screened pad mount transformer, and the proposal’s vehicular entrance and large vehicle refuse/servicing bay. The ground level contains activated retail space, the upper half of the double storey pad mount transformer enclosure and screened carparking. Level 1 accommodates 3 x 2 bedroom apartments, and their street fronting private balconies. Overall, the podium form has a similar architectural treatment to the new podium form fronting Leichhardt Street; a landscaped colonnade at basement level 1, and the continuation of those columns up through, full frontage width planters at ground level and level 1. At all levels the planters provide a subtropical screen and edging to the building. The podium element’s parapet, which has a height alignment just lower than the parapet of the heritage building is similarly lined with a landscaped planter box. The planter box both conceals and provides an edge to a podium top residential courtyards at level 2.”

[28]I accept Dr Robinson’s evidence. It is made good having regard to the evidence referred to at

[24]. Dr Robinson’s evidence is also made good having regard to Figures NR15 to 18 of exhibit 9 to the Burrell appeal.

[29]The project architects retained by Dibcorp have employed a number of design strategies to give the podium a visually interesting streetscape appearance. The design strategies, which contribute to a cohesive streetscape, were identified by Dr Robinson in these terms: “i. The colonnade’s rhythm and the striated use of planters at each level provide the new podium element with a level of detail and human scale that references the detail level and very human scaling of the hotel building… ii. The new 12m wide podium element facing Leichhardt Street is approximately the same width and height as the hotel, creating a continuation of the streetscape scale. The new 30m wide podium element facing Little Edward Street, although twice the width of the hotel, relies on its height which is equivalent to the hotel and its colonnade, and play of light grey columns and shadowy recesses to create a fine grained, human scaled street presentation.”

[30]I accept Dr Robinson’s evidence. It is made good by the evidence identified at

[24]. The evidence depicts a visually interesting podium that will provide a cohesive human scale interface with two street frontages.

[31]Overall, the photomontages demonstrate that: (a) the podium, as a whole, will provide an attractive visual base to the building and facilitate a height transition from the tower to the pedestrian environment at street level; (b) the new part of the podium creates an attractive and activated green edge along two street frontages (provided by planters with cascading vegetation); (c) the new part of the podium integrates with the retained hotel façade in an appropriate and respectful way – the ‘new’ appropriately defers to, and is distinguished from, the ‘old’; and (d) the new podium allows the retained hotel façade to be a focal point at pedestrian level, making a positive contribution to the streetscape of this part of Spring Hill.

[32]The maximum site cover for the podium is 82% (at level 2).

[33]A 14 storey residential apartment tower sits on top of the podium form. It has an overall length of 40.3 metres along Little Edward Street and 26.3 metres along Leichhardt Street. The stacked apartment floor plates are in the order of 909m 2 in size, resulting in a site cover at levels 3 to 15 in the order of 57.6%.

[34]Each face of the tower is marked at its midpoint with a vertical recess extending from the top of the podium to the roof. The recesses vary in depth. The shallowest is on the southern façade (0.7 metres deep). The deepest recess is on the northern façade (2.3 metres deep).

[35]The tower façades are articulated by a combination of recessed balconies, a 300mm wide concrete sunhood, floor to ceiling glazing and portions of solid façade with a ribbed finish.

[36]The boundary setbacks for the tower at the four points of the compass are as follows (Ex.8, para 29): (a) Level 3 – 4.3m north, 3.5m east, 6m south and 4.25m west; (b) Level 4 to 13 – 4.5m north, 3.5m east, 5.7m south and 4.55m west; (c) Level 14 to 15 – 4.2m north, 3.2m east, 5.7m south and 4.25m west; and (d) Rooftop – 4.2m north, 3.2m east, 5.7m south and 4.25m west.

[37]Dr Robinson identified the architectural design strategies employed to manage and minimise the bulk of the tower. At paragraph 71 and 72 of the Architecture joint report, he observed: “71. The proposal’s tower has incorporated the following architectural strategies to manage and minimise intrinsic building bulk – which I have found to be successful: i. The northern, eastern, southern and western façades of the tower are marked at their respective midpoints by a vertical recess running the height of the tower…This visually breaks down the building scale, dividing each of the building’s facades into two narrower vertical elements, an effect that becomes more pronounced when the building is viewed in parallax and when the sun permits deep shadowing effect. The recesses break the tower into a collection of 4 slender ‘mini towers’. Interestingly the width of the mini towers is similar to that of the hotel. This compatibility effectively and appropriately transposes a degree of the finer streetscape texture into the form and articulation of the tower. ii. At the next level of articulation the tower has been punctured with recessed balconies. The resultant shadow effects provide visual variety and texture to the mini towers. The recessed balconies on the north-east, north- west and south-east corners of the tower floor plates create visual movement and human scaled texture up the building’s edges, visually shortening the building façades by creating edge transparency and enhancing view lines past the building. iii. A 300mm wide concrete sunhood has then been provided around the full perimeter of each floor plate, formed as an extension of each levels concrete floor slab. The applicant has included this feature as a horizontal sunshade, but it also serves to visually break up any potentially large vertical building surfaces and will create more shadow driven textural play to the façades. iv. As a final layer of bulk mitigation, the remaining solid facade portions of the ‘mini towers’ have been broken up through striped interplay of floor to ceiling glazing and ribbed off form concrete panels. The ribbing detail provides visual texture through the rippling effect of shadowed and lit surfaces. 72. Cumulatively the architectural strategies employed have in my opinion successfully managed and minimised the tower’s intrinsic building bulk by breaking up the tower’s mass into a series of 4 highly textured smaller vertical mini towers. The use of façade recess, material finish, shadow effects and banding are particularly effective, and the resultant tower incorporates human scaling and is integrated and compatible with the finer grain scale of the existing hotel building and streetscape…” (emphasis added)

[38]Paragraph 71ii above identifies a feature of the design that creates ‘edge transparency’. This feature is not particularly prominent in the photomontages and is a reference to the effect created by the permeable recessed balconies on the edges of the tower. Mr Curtis described the edge effect in this way: “88. The floor plates include recessed balconies at their northwest, northeast and southeast corners. The vertical ‘stacking’ of the floor plates allows the consistent corner balconies to ‘erode’ and provide a ‘feathering’ effect at the corners of the tower, which assist to mitigate visual impact of the tower’s bulk.”

[39]Save for the parts of the evidence emphasised above, I accept Dr Robinson’s evidence. I also accept Mr Curtis’ evidence. The evidence accords with the photomontages.

[40]As to the emphasised parts of Dr Robinson’s evidence, while I accept the design of the proposal incorporates elements that successfully mitigate the bulk of the structure, the visual result is more subtle than the phrase ‘4 highly textured mini towers’ conveys. As I have already said, the façades are marked at their respective midpoints with a recess. The depth of each recess varies depending upon the façade. This aspect of the design can be seen in the photomontages and is successful in mitigating building bulk and breaking the tower into discernible elements. However, it is an overstatement to suggest the façades present as 4 mini towers. On the northern and eastern façades, the effect of the recessing is visible but subtle (Ex.6, pp.34, 46 and 50). It is even less prominent on the southern and western façades. Those façades are successfully broken up at the midpoint by the vertical recess, but not to the degree that they can be described as ‘mini towers’ (Ex.6, pp.38 and 42).

[41]The roof level of the proposed development accommodates services and communal recreation space for the apartments. The facilities include an enclosed dining room; BBQ and undercover terrace; two plunge pools; turfed area; and a sauna and toilet amenities. The recreation space is screened at the building perimeter by raised landscaped planters. The roof has the appearance of a green edge.

[42]The landscaping regime proposed for the development is discussed at s 2.7.2 of the Visual amenity and landscape architecture joint report. It comprises: (1) two small pockets of low shrubs either side of the driveway crossover to Little Edward Street; (2) containerised planting above the driveway crossover and small retail tenancy with frontage to Little Edward Street; (3) in ground and containerised planting on each side of the pedestrian entry off Leichhardt Street, extending to an internal courtyard; (4) two street trees and low level planting in the verge of Leichhardt Street; (5) street trees in the verge of Little Edward Street; (6) containerised and cascading planting to the face of the podium; and (7) containerised planting around the communal open space on the roof terrace level.

[43]Mr Brooksby, who is a landscape architect, described the proposed landscape treatments and their appearance in the photomontages: Ex.11, Yi Shiu appeal, s 3.4.1. His description is fulsome and I do not intend to repeat it. It is sufficient to say that Mr Brooksby’s observations about the proposed landscaping regime are made good having regard to the proposed landscape plans and photomontages. I accept his evidence. The landscaping proposed is attractive, particularly on the ground level and on the faces of the podium. In combination with street trees, the proposed landscaping will make a positive contribution to the streetscape of this part of Spring Hill.

[44]Vehicle access, including access for service vehicles, is obtained at level B1 of the proposed development from Little Edward Street. The access is configured so the driveway for service vehicles is separated from the driveway for vehicles accessing the basement carpark. A total of 137 car parking spaces are provided across five levels. Statutory assessment framework

[45]The statutory assessment framework for this appeal is prescribed by the PA. The PA requires the development application be assessed in accordance with s 45(5) and decided in accordance with ss 59(3) and 60.

[46]Section 45(5)(a)(i) calls for an assessment, to the extent relevant, against assessment benchmarks in a categorising instrument. Section 45(7) confirms this is a reference to an assessment benchmark in effect when the development application was properly made. Version 28 of City Plan 2014 (the planning scheme) was in force at this time. Version 28 has since been amended. The amendments have no role to play in this appeal and do not require consideration of the kind contemplated by s 45(8) of the PA.

[47]Section 45(5)(a)(ii) requires an assessment manager, and this Court on appeal (by operation of s 46(2)(a) of the Planning and Environment Court Act 2016), to have regard to matters prescribed by regulation. One such matter is the common material (s 31(1)(g) of the Planning Regulation 2017). This material includes submissions. Council received 6 submissions. The town planning witnesses prepared an agreed summary of the issues raised in the submissions, both for and against approval. After reading the submissions, I accept the town planning witnesses have accurately summarised the issues raised. Their helpful summary is in the following terms: “30. The development application received six submissions, four in opposition and two in support. The matters raised in the submissions are summarised as follows: (a) Aspects of the restoration of the heritage building, including the desirability of reinstating the former veranda to the street façades and concern about the new tower visually dominating and not sensitively interacting with the heritage building; (b) Overdevelopment of the site, with additional building height inconsistent with performance outcome PO1 of the Spring Hill neighbourhood plan code, relying on development intentions and approvals for a different precinct of the neighbourhood plan from that in which the site is located; (c) Inadequate building setbacks together with side facing balconies, to the detriment of redevelopment opportunities for adjoining premises; and (d) Inadequate landscaping and sub-tropical design character due to the absence of deep planting.”

[48]There is significant overlap with the issues in dispute and subparagraphs (a) to (d) above. The submissions, in so far as they speak to the disputed issues, are entitled to weight and have informed the assessment of the development application. The submissions have also been considered in conjunction with lay witness statements tendered in the appeal, which are also entitled to weight and have informed the assessment. For reasons that will become clear, the adverse issues raised in the submissions and lay witness statements, while relevant, do not call for refusal here. The substance of the submissions and lay witness statements are not supported by the evidence I accept.

[49]The statutory assessment framework is to be approached in accordance with the following Court of Appeal authorities, namely: Brisbane City Council v YQ Property Pty Ltd

[2021] QPELR 987; Abeleda v Brisbane City Council (2020) 6 QR 441; Wilhelm v Logan City Council & Ors

[2021] QPELR 1321; and Trinity Park Investments Pty Ltd v Cairns Regional Council & Ors; Dexus Funds Management Ltd v Fabcot Pty Ltd & Ors

[2022] QPELR 309. The authorities confirm, among other things, that: (1) in contrast to its statutory predecessor, the discretion conferred by s 60(3) of the PA admits of more flexibility to approve an application in the face of non-compliance with a planning scheme; and (2) a planning scheme remains a reflection of the public interest. Planning scheme context

[50]The planning scheme includes the site in: (1) the Inner city zone precinct of the Mixed use zone; and (2) the Boundary Street heart precinct of the SHNPC.

[51]The primary focus of the refusal case is the SHNPC: Ex.63, para 1. It provides the most locally focused planning for the site and locality. The specific nature of the planning articulated in the SHNPC means an assessment against it will attract significant, and potentially decisive, weight in the exercise of the planning discretion.

[52]‘Neighbourhood plans’ are found in Part 7 of the planning scheme. They address matters ‘at the local or district level and may provide more detailed planning for the zones’: s 7.1, 1. This general statement is consistent with the purpose of the SHNPC, which is to provide ‘finer grained’ planning at a ‘local level’ for the Spring Hill neighbourhood plan area: s 7.2.19.5.2, 1.

[53]While neighbourhood plans provide finer grained planning at a local level, it does not follow that zone provisions cease to have a role to play. Neighbourhood plans build on and complement underlying zoning. This is evident from: (1) the verbiage of the SHNPC and associated mapping; and (2) the Mixed use zone code (the zone code). The zone code has application to the site and the development application. Reference to that document confirms the zone, irrespective of where it occurs, is ‘fully covered’ by neighbourhood plans. The neighbourhood plans contain specific provisions responding to the individual roles and characteristics of the areas that comprise the zone: s 6.2.6.4, 1, 2nd Note.

[54]Given the SHNPC builds on the zone code, it is useful to commence an examination of the planning context with brief reference to the latter in order to ‘set the scene’.

[55]The zone code states the purpose of the Mixed use zone. The purpose is to provide for a variety of uses and activities, including business and residential uses: s 6.2.6.4, 1. The purpose of the zone will be achieved through a number of overall outcomes.

[56]Overall outcome 3 is directed to the ‘Zone role’. The role of the zone is to support the implementation of specific provisions of the Strategic framework: OO 3a. It is unnecessary to dwell on the provisions of the Strategic framework.

[57]Overall outcome 4 deals with ‘Development location and uses’. The overall outcome confirms that development in the zone provides a mix of uses, including commercial, retail and residential uses: OO 4a. The preferred mix of uses is to be, inter alia: (1) tailored to the specific location, role and function of an area: OO 4aii; and (2) consistent with the location-specific provisions in a relevant neighbourhood plan: OO4 aiii. Residential land uses are anticipated in the zone. Given the mixed use nature of the zone, residents cannot expect to enjoy the same level of amenity as the low density suburban areas: OO 4i.

[58]Overall outcome 5 deals with ‘Development form’. The intended form in the zone is to be of a ‘height, bulk, scale and form’ tailored to its specific location and characteristics of the site, including the nature of the surrounding neighbourhood and proximity to public transport: OO 5a. Development is also to respect existing heritage, and provide built form that creates a consistent and cohesive landscape and streetscape.

[59]As I have already observed, the site is included in the Inner city zone precinct of the Mixed use zone. The overall outcomes for the zone precinct are as follows (overall outcome 6 of the zone code): “a.Development capitalises on proximity to the City centre and inner-city specialised centres and Special purpose zone precincts in the Inner city zone precinct. b.Development provides for a specific mix of uses that respond to a particular location within the inner city and ensure that the use is appropriate given its proximity to the City Centre and surrounding specialised centres, Special purpose zone precincts and residential areas.” (emphasis added)

[60]Against the background of the above, attention can turn to the SHNPC. It has a familiar structure, comprising a stated purpose; overall outcomes that achieve the purpose; overall outcomes for specific precincts; and a table of performance and acceptable outcomes, which are intended to achieve the overall outcomes of the neighbourhood plan code. Compliance is demonstrated with the SHNPC where development complies with the purpose, overall outcomes and performance outcomes: ss 7.2.19.5.1, 3 and 5.3.3, 4c and 5.

[61]Land subject to the SHNPC is identified on planning scheme map NPM-019.5 Spring Hill neighbourhood plan map: s 7.2.19.5.2. An extract of the map is included in exhibit 3. It identifies an area situated, moving anti-clockwise from the north, between the Inner City Bypass, Roma Street Parkland, the CBD and Fortitude Valley. With these features in mind, it can be said that the neighbourhood plan area is well located to a range of facilities, including employment nodes, retail uses, high frequency public transport, hospitals and schools.

[62]Thirteen overall outcomes are identified for the SHNPC as a whole: s 7.2.19.5.2, 3. The first three (OO 3a, b and c) describe the existing and intended character of Spring Hill. Overall outcome 3a describes Spring Hill as a diverse neighbourhood, with ‘higher density mixed use activities’ bordering, inter alia, the CBD and ‘along major streets’. This description, along with overall outcomes 3b and c, accurately recognise the diversity of existing land uses and built form in Spring Hill: see

[11] to

[17].

[63]The remaining overall outcomes (OO 3d to m) appear to speak prospectively in their terms, guiding new development. The overall outcomes having a role to play in the refusal case are as follows (Ex.63, Annexure A, item 1): “f. Development reinforces the diverse architecture and urban form that sets Spring Hill apart from other parts of Brisbane and is responsive to each site’s specific shape, size, topography, context and setting. g. Development is designed to take advantage of Brisbane’s subtropical climate and delivers high quality, subtropical architecture. Buildings, the public realm and landscaped spaces are designed to be open, engaging and green, with shaded spaces and opportunities to interact with the street, and contribute to Brisbane’s identity and lifestyle. … i. Development in the…Mixed use zone…provides slender towers that are sited and designed to maintain the openness of street vistas with adequate spacing between buildings to allow for light penetration, air circulation, views and vistas, and privacy, particularly for residential towers.” (emphasis added)

[64]The meaning of ‘subtropical architecture’ and ‘slender towers’ is determined having regard to s 1.3.1, 1 of the planning scheme, which states: “1. A term used in the planning scheme has the meaning assigned to that term by one of the following: a. the Planning Act 2016 (the Act); b. the Planning Regulation 2017 (the Regulation); c. the definitions in Schedule 1 of the planning scheme; d. the Acts Interpretation Act 1954; e. the ordinary meaning where that term is not defined in any of the above.

[65]I was not directed to, nor could I find definitions of slender, tower, slender towers or subtropical architecture in the documents referred to in s 1.3.1, 1a to d above. As a consequence, subparagraph e is engaged; the emphasised phrases in paragraph

[63] are to be given their ‘ordinary meaning’.

[66]What is the ordinary meaning of ‘subtropical architecture’?

[67]In the context of overall outcome 3g, the ordinary meaning of the phrase, in my view, is simply stated: the phrase describes a form of architectural typology. The defining feature of this typology is its response to subtropical climate considerations. For a subtropical climate such as Brisbane, climatic design responses can be evident in building orientation; the provision of indoor/outdoor living opportunities; landscaping; and responses to heat load, weather protection (rain and sun) and access to natural light and breezes. This is confirmed, in my view, by the language of performance outcomes PO5 and PO6 of the SHNPC. The latter requires development to exhibit ‘best-practice climate responsive design, including orientation, to mitigate heat and reduce the need for mechanical heating, cooling and lighting’.

[68]Additional contextual support can be found in Table 7.2.19.5.3.C – Urban context report. The table is called up in an ‘Editor’s note’ to the SHNPC. The ‘note’ provides that an Urban context report may be used as a vehicle to demonstrate ‘how the proposal achieves the outcomes’ of the SHNPC. A topic to be considered in the report is ‘Subtropical climate’. This part of the report is used to demonstrate the following: “…how the development design incorporates orientation, shading, outdoor spaces, natural ventilation, landscaping and articulation to reduce heat loading, protect from weather, optimise natural light and support outdoor lifestyles.”

[69]The matters to be demonstrated in the Urban context report for ‘Subtropical climate’ have a nexus with performance outcomes PO5 and PO6 of the SHNPC.

[70]What is the ordinary meaning of the phrase ‘slender towers’?

[71]This question is to be answered by considering, in the first instance, the meaning of the word ‘tower’, followed then by the meaning of ‘slender’.

[72]As a matter of ordinary experience, a tower is readily identifiable. It is a building where the overall height is greater in proportion to its lateral dimensions. The relativity between the height and lateral dimensions of the form may yield a tower that is tall and slender in appearance. In other cases, it may not; the relativity may be such that the built form has a squat, stubby or block like appearance.

[73]Where, as here, a building has a podium and tower form, it is necessary to consider the adopted planning controls to identify the point at which the controls delineate between the respective forms for assessment purposes. The SHNPC offers clear guidance about the point of delineation. It has in mind that a tower is the part of a building sitting above, and set back from the edge of, a ‘street building’. In this regard, a ‘Note’ to the SHNPC states (s 7.2.19.5.1): “…The ‘street building’ comprises all levels of a building below which a set back tower commences. Refer to Figure c for guidance.”

[74]Reference to Figure c, which is called up in acceptable outcome AO4.1 of the SHNPC, draws a distinction between a ‘street building’ and a ‘tower above’. The figure confirms that a tower component sits on, and is setback from the edge of, a street building below.

[75]A review of the proposed design reveals a street building is proposed. It has a predominant setback of zero metres to the street frontages and ceases at the floor slab for level 2. At the same point, there is a tower form that rises out of the level 2 floor slab. The tower is setback from the edges of the street building below and extends up to and including the roof level. This is the tower component of the proposal. No party contended otherwise.

[76]Turning to the word ‘slender’, it precedes and qualifies the word ‘towers’ in overall outcome 3i. Its presence means a type of tower is anticipated, namely one that has a particular relationship between its height and lateral dimensions. The ordinary meaning of the word slender conveys that the relationship anticipated is one of disproportion. Overall outcome 3i does not identify, in empirical terms, the extent of disproportion required to demonstrate compliance. In the absence of this, it is necessary to turn to the SHNPC for context. That is to be found in performance outcomes, acceptable outcomes and overall outcome 3i of the SHNPC.

[77]For a neighbourhood plan code, performance outcomes are intended to achieve the overall outcomes of the code: s 7.1, 6e. To achieve an overall outcome such as 3i, Table 7.2.19.5.3.A sets out the performance outcomes and acceptable outcomes for the SHNPC. Performance outcomes PO1 and PO3, and their associated acceptable outcomes, are directed towards building height, scale and form in the neighbourhood plan area. Compliance with performance outcome PO1 is achieved by, inter alia, compliance with the number of storeys stated in Table 7.2.19.5.3.B of the SHNPC (AO1). Compliance with performance outcome PO3 is achieved by, inter alia, compliance with the setbacks stated in Table 7.2.19.5.3.D (AO3.1) and maximum tower site cover in Table 7.2.19.5.3.B (AO3.2). The metrics stated in the two tables enable a building envelope to be identified for the site.

[78]Taking the height and boundary setbacks prescribed in the tables above in conjunction with the site size and overall dimensions (identified at

[14]), a compliant building envelope for the purpose of acceptable outcomes AO1, AO3.1 and AO3.2 can be identified. It has the following features: 10 storeys in height; zero metre setback at lower levels to create a street building; side and rear setbacks of 6 metres for a tower; and a front tower setback of 3 metres to any balcony and 6 metres to a wall. These combined metrics yield a building envelope in the order of 30 metres high (assuming 3 metres floor-to-floor height), with a tower commencing at the top of level 2 and with lateral dimensions of 23 metres x 39 metres (taking the frontage widths in

[14], less a 2 x 6 metre setback). The resulting three dimensional shape created by the building envelope is a long rectangular stubby block sitting upon, and set back from, a podium below. This follows, in my view, because one horizontal plane of the building envelope is greater in length than its height, while the other horizontal plane is shorter than the height, but not significantly so. The relationship between the height and lateral dimensions of this hypothetical building envelope suggests, in my view, that compliance with overall outcome 3i is not necessarily precluded because a tower fails, after a visual assessment, to exhibit a marked level of disproportion between its height and lateral dimensions. A visual assessment is therefore not the start and finish of the exercise.

[79]That a visual assessment is not the start and finish of the exercise is confirmed by the tail end of overall outcome 3i. This part of the provision identifies a planning purpose for slender towers in the neighbourhood plan area. The planning purpose bears repeating and is in the following terms: “…slender towers that are sited and designed to maintain the openness of street vistas with adequate spacing between buildings to allow for light penetration, air circulation, views and vistas, and privacy, particularly for residential towers.”

[80]A tower that has been sited and designed to achieve the planning purpose is well on its way to demonstrating compliance with overall outcome 3i or, at worst, demonstrating that any non-compliance with the provision is technical by reason of the absence of adverse planning consequences. To arrive at either point, consideration will be given to the three dimensional proportions of the tower and a compliant site building envelope. Care should however be taken not to focus on these two matters unduly. To do so risks placing too greater emphasis on the three dimensions of the tower, and too little emphasis on the extent of separation from, and impact on, other buildings and street vistas.

[81]The verbiage of overall outcome 3i calls for close and careful scrutiny of the design of a proposed tower to examine how it has been sited and designed to provide separation, that is, ‘breathing space’ around it. An assessment against the planning purpose may produce a range of results, including a conclusion that a tower, despite the absence of a marked difference in its three dimensional proportions, complies with the stated planning purpose. While the conclusion reached will of course turn on the facts and circumstances of each case, generally, good planning reasons would need to be identified to conclude that a tower, which complies with the planning purpose of overall outcome 3i, is not sufficiently ‘slender’ for the neighbourhood plan area.

[82]For completeness, it can be observed that Council contended the phrase ‘slender towers’ should be given a meaning other than its ordinary meaning. It went so far as to suggest that the building envelope produced by application of the acceptable outcomes for height and setbacks ‘should be taken to be one that is considered “slender” in compliance with the overall outcome’: Ex.64, para 11. While this approach is superficially attractive, I have two misgivings about adopting such a course.

[83]First, I have difficulty accepting that a building envelope, in and of itself, is a proxy for compliance with the planning purpose of overall outcome 3i given: (1) the metrics that create the building envelope are not called up by overall outcome 3i, or any other provision that speaks of a slender tower form; and (2) for the reasons at

[80].

[84]Second, Council’s submission does not sit comfortably with s 1.3.1, 1e of the planning scheme. This provision requires a term used in the document to be given its ordinary meaning in the absence of a definition in one of three instruments – there are no such definitions for ‘slender’, ‘tower’ or ‘slender towers’. Regrettably, Council’s written submissions did not mention s 1.3.1, 1e in this context. Nor did the submissions identify authority supportive of the proposition that s 1.3.1, 1e can be displaced when construing a provision such as overall outcome 3i of the SHNPC.

[85]The neighbourhood plan area is divided into 4 precincts (Ex.3, p.14): (1) City Centre expansion precinct; (2) Spring Hill mixed use precinct; (3) Boundary Street heart precinct; and (4) Spring Hill east precinct. The SHNPC contains overall outcomes for each precinct.

[86]Before dealing with overall outcomes for the Boundary Street heart precinct, it can be observed that the SHNPC, read as a whole, reflects two deliberate planning decisions of relevance.

[87]The first deliberate decision is reflected in Table 7.2.19.5.3.B of the SHNPC. A review of the table, which informs building height in the neighbourhood plan area, reveals the amalgamation of land is encouraged and incentivised.

[88]The evidence did not suggest there is a clear reason connecting site area and associated building heights in Table 7.2.19.5.3.B. This is not to say the site area ranges in the table have no underlying design rationale; experience suggests it will play a part. It is however, in my view, unlikely to be the only or primary explanation for the site area ranges. The more likely explanation is the existing pattern of subdivision. The pattern of subdivision can be seen on zoning maps, which are cadastrally based. A review of the cadastre, and resulting lot sizes, suggests the site area ranges in Table 7.2.19.5.3.B reflect an intent to address a highly fragmented existing cadastral base.

[89]The site the subject of this appeal is an amalgamated development parcel.

[90]The second deliberate decision is one that admits of a forward planning strategy.

[91]The most intense development in the neighbourhood plan area is anticipated in the City Centre expansion precinct. This precinct adjoins the CBD. Built form is intended to provide a transition in height between the CBD and development in the Boundary Street heart and Spring Hill mixed use precincts: OO 4a. Table 7.2.19.5.3.B, which is called up by acceptable outcomes AO1 and AO3.2 of the SHNPC, anticipates buildings in the City Centre expansion precinct that range from 3 to 30 storeys, depending on site size. Performance outcome PO4 of the SHNPC also requires development in the precinct to include a ‘street building’.

[92]That the City Centre expansion precinct is intended to accommodate the most intense form of development is confirmed by two identical overall outcomes for the Spring Hill mixed use and Boundary Street heart precincts. Development in these precincts must be of a height and scale subordinate to development in the City Centre expansion precinct: OO 5a and 6b. Table 7.2.19.5.3.B anticipates buildings in the Spring Hill mixed use precinct that range from 3 to 20 storeys, depending on site size, underlying zoning and inclusion in a sub-precinct. Performance outcome PO4 requires development in the precinct and included in the Mixed use zone to include a ‘street building’. Buildings in the Boundary Street heart precinct range from 3 to 15 storeys, depending on site size and underlying zoning. Performance outcome PO4 requires development in the Mixed use zone to incorporate a ‘street building’. This captures a significant part of the Boundary Street heart precinct that has frontage to Leichhardt Street, including land in the Mixed use zone between Leichhardt Street/Fortescue Street/Boundary Street/Little Edward Street.

[93]Development in the Spring Hill east precinct is not subject to an express requirement that it be subordinate to development in the City Centre expansion precinct. This is explained by the underlying zoning; land at the south-western edge of the precinct, which is to the north-east and removed from the City Centre expansion precinct, is included in a lower density residential zone. The north-eastern part of the precinct, where included in the Mixed use zone, is intended to provide a transition between lower density residential development to the higher density areas of Bowen Hills: OO 7d. Table 7.2.19.5.3.B anticipates buildings in the precinct that range from 3 to 20 storeys, depending on site size, underlying zoning and the location of its primary frontage.

[94]The site sits at the interface of three precincts: the Boundary Street heart precinct, the Spring Hill mixed use precinct and the City Centre expansion precinct. The Boundary Street heart precinct, where the site is located, is separated from the other two precincts by Leichhardt Street. The third deliberate decision reflected in the SHNPC goes to the design of new built form at this interface.

[95]While the SHNPC requires development to be subordinate in height and scale, the document does not require a specific design response at the interface between the three precincts at the intersection of Leichhardt Street and Little Edward Street. Nor does it require a transition within the Boundary Street heart or Spring Hill mixed use precincts down to its edges. This is in circumstances where: (1) local topography is undulating, resulting in a fragmented skyline in, and between, precincts of the SHNPC; (2) the northern boundary between the City Centre expansion precinct follows the meandering alignment of Leichhardt Street, which, from some vantage points, causes a blurring of precinct boundaries (built form in different precincts appears to merge and clump: Ex.6, pp.25 and 37); and (3) the local planning authority has turned its mind to the management of interfaces in the neighbourhood plan area but does not require the creation of hard or stepped edges that serve to mark a precinct boundary. With respect to (3), this is evident in the following provisions of the SHNPC, namely: (a) a ‘Note’ in s 7.2.19.5.1 of the SHNPC, which states that the building height transitions provisions as identified in the Multiple dwelling code and/or the Centre or mixed use code do not apply; (b) overall outcome 3e for the neighbourhood plan area, which requires development to provide a ‘sensitive design response’ to character dwellings, heritage places and commercial character buildings; (c) overall outcome 3j for the neighbourhood plan area, which requires development in particular zones, including the Mixed use zone, to be ‘set back from’, and provide a ‘highly landscaped design response’ to adjoining sites in residential zones, including the Character residential zone; (d) overall outcome 4a for the City Centre expansion precinct, which speaks of development comprising a mix of uses that provide a ‘transition in height’ between the CBD and development in the Boundary Street heart and Spring Hill mixed use precincts; (e) overall outcome 5b for the Spring Hill mixed use precinct, which requires development in, inter alia, the Mixed use zone to be ‘set back from’, and provide a ‘highly landscaped design response’ to adjoining sites in a residential zone; (f) overall outcome 6c for the Boundary Street heart precinct, which requires development in, inter alia, the Mixed use zone to be ‘set back from’, and provide a ‘highly landscaped design response’ to adjoining sites in a residential zone; (g) overall outcome 7b for the Spring Hill east precinct, which requires development in, inter alia, the Mixed use zone to be ‘set back from’, and provide a ‘highly landscaped design response’ to adjoining sites in a residential zone; (h) overall outcome 7d for the Spring Hill east precinct, which requires development in the Mixed use zone to provide a ‘transition’ to lower density residential development; and (i) overall outcome 7d for the Spring Hill east precinct, which states in part: “…Development is of a higher intensity along St Pauls Terrace, to provide a height transition to the Principal centre zoned land on the opposite side of the road.”

[96]The absence of an overall outcome like 7d for the Boundary Street heart precinct, in my view, is deliberate. It conveys that a specific height transition is not required internal to, or at the edge of, the Boundary Street heart precinct where it shares a common boundary with the City Centre expansion or Spring Hill mixed use precincts.

[97]The appellants’ architect, Mr Richards, appears to have adopted a different approach to the SHNPC. His examination of the proposal, and subsequent opinions about compliance with the neighbourhood plan, assume the SHNPC requires a ‘clear contrast’ between built form on either side of the interface between the Boundary Street heart and City Centre expansion precincts. This contention seemed to imply that a transition in height, and scale, was required within the Boundary Street heart precinct, reducing in height from its centre to the edges, to achieve a clear contrast at the interface point. To the extent Mr Richards’ evidence about the acceptability of the proposal and compliance with the SHNPC was premised on such an assumption, I do not accept it.

[98]Turning to the overall outcomes for the Boundary Street heart precinct, they are in the following terms (overall outcome 6): “a. Development in the District centre zone reinforces Boundary Street as the ‘heart’ of Spring Hill through support for a mix of uses and as a location for higher-order services. b. Development is of a height and scale that is subordinate to development in the City Centre expansion precinct. c. Development in the District centre zone or Mixed use zone is set back from, and provides a highly landscaped design response to adjoining sites in the Character residential zone or Low-medium density residential zone to minimise privacy and amenity impacts on residents. d. Development in the Mixed use zone supports existing community facilities in the area through the promotion of complementary uses, including Health care service and Short- term accommodation. e. Development promotes Boundary Street as a pedestrian friendly street through a combination of ground level activation, high quality street building and streetscape design, and welcoming front entrances.”

[99]The appellants contend there is non-compliance with overall outcome 6b. No other non-compliance is asserted with the precinct specific overall outcomes.

[100]Overall outcome 6b for the precinct requires development to be ‘subordinate to’ development in the City Centre expansion precinct. No party contended this planning objective is unsoundly based or overtaken by events. This is correct. The planning reflects an identifiable difference between the existing built form in Spring Hill and the CBD. For reasons that follow, an approval here will not change this position; the identifiable difference in built form between the precincts will remain.

[101]The term ‘subordinate’ in overall outcome 6b is not defined and, as a consequence, is to be given its plain meaning. In a built form context, and with overall outcome 4a of the SHNPC in mind, the term places a constraint on the three dimensional presentation of development (height and scale) in the Boundary Street heart precinct to achieve a transition to the south across precincts, bookended by the CBD. Overall outcome 6b does not however provide a clear test for how, or when, this will be achieved in the Boundary Street heart precinct despite items (1), (2) and (3) at

[95]. As a consequence, I accept the appellants’ submission that compliance with overall outcome 6b in any given case will turn on matters of fact, degree and impression: Ex.63, paras 5 and 7.

[102]When examining matters of fact and degree, I accept the appellants’ submission that the assessment is assisted by the expected height differential between the precincts, which is evident from Table 7.2.19.5.3.B; the table provides relevant context: Ex.63, paras 23 and 118. I also accept the assessment should take into account existing features of the locality, including topography: Ex.63, para 34.

[103]Table 7.2.19.5.3.A contains performance outcomes and acceptable outcomes for the SHNPC. Performance outcomes PO1 to PO7 apply to land in, inter alia, the Mixed use zone. The appellants contend there is non-compliance with performance outcomes PO1 and PO3: Ex.63, Annexure A, items 3 and 4. These provisions, including the associated acceptable outcomes are, in part, as follows: PO1 Development is of a height, scale and form that achieves the intended outcome for the precinct, improves the amenity of the neighbourhood AO1 Development complies with the number of storeys in Table 7.2.19.5.3.B – Maximum building height and maximum tower site plan area, contributes to a cohesive streetscape and built form character and is: a. consistent with anticipated density and assumed infrastructure demand; b. aligned to community expectations about the number of storeys to be built; c. proportionate and commensurate with the utility of the site area and frontage width; d. designed to avoid significant and undue adverse amenity impact to adjoining development; e. sited to enable existing and future buildings to be well separated from each other and to avoid affecting the potential development of an adjoining site. … Note––Development that is over- scaled for its site can result in an undesirable dominance of vehicle access, parking and manoeuvring areas that significantly reduce streetscape character and amenity. cover. … And: PO3 Development is of a scale and form that contributes to a cohesive streetscape and built form character, and: a. does not cause significant and undue adverse amenity impacts to adjoining properties, or prejudice their development; b. is sited and designed to enable existing and future buildings to be well separated from each other to allow for light penetration, air circulation between buildings, and the preservation of views, vistas AO3.1 Development ensures setbacks are in accordance with Table 7.2.19.5.3.D – Boundary setback requirements. AO3.2 Development has a maximum tower site cover in accordance with Table 7.2.19.5.3.B – Maximum building height and maximum tower site cover. … and resident privacy; c. protects the amenity of properties located in the Character residential zone or Low-medium density residential zone; d. respects the existing built form of properties identified in the Heritage overlay, Pre- 1911 building overlay, and the Commercial character building overlay.

[104]The proposed development does not comply with acceptable outcomes AO1 and AO3.1. An alternative performance based solution is advanced by Dibcorp. The terms of each performance outcome is, therefore, the focus of the assessment.

[105]The refusal cases advanced on behalf of the appellants did not assert non-compliance with performance outcomes PO1a or PO3c: Ex.63, Annexure A, items 3 and 4. These parts of the performance outcomes have therefore been struck through where they appear in the above extract.

[106]One disputed issue in the Burrell appeal requires reference to the Transport, access, parking and servicing code (TAPS code) and the planning scheme policy of the same name (TAPS policy). The provisions of the code and policy are traversed later in these reasons for judgment.
