(f) overall outcome 6c for the Boundary Street heart precinct, which requires development in, inter alia, the Mixed use zone to be ‘set back from’, and provide a ‘highly landscaped design response’ to adjoining sites in a residential zone; (g) overall outcome 7b for the Spring Hill east precinct, which requires development in, inter alia, the Mixed use zone to be ‘set back from’, and provide a ‘highly landscaped design response’ to adjoining sites in a residential zone; (h) overall outcome 7d for the Spring Hill east precinct, which requires development in the Mixed use zone to provide a ‘transition’ to lower density residential development; and (i) overall outcome 7d for the Spring Hill east precinct, which states in part: “…Development is of a higher intensity along St Pauls Terrace, to provide a height transition to the Principal centre zoned land on the opposite side of the road.”

[96]The absence of an overall outcome like 7d for the Boundary Street heart precinct, in my view, is deliberate. It conveys that a specific height transition is not required internal to, or at the edge of, the Boundary Street heart precinct where it shares a common boundary with the City Centre expansion or Spring Hill mixed use precincts.

[97]The appellants’ architect, Mr Richards, appears to have adopted a different approach to the SHNPC. His examination of the proposal, and subsequent opinions about compliance with the neighbourhood plan, assume the SHNPC requires a ‘clear contrast’ between built form on either side of the interface between the Boundary Street heart and City Centre expansion precincts. This contention seemed to imply that a transition in height, and scale, was required within the Boundary Street heart precinct, reducing in height from its centre to the edges, to achieve a clear contrast at the interface point. To the extent Mr Richards’ evidence about the acceptability of the proposal and compliance with the SHNPC was premised on such an assumption, I do not accept it.

[98]Turning to the overall outcomes for the Boundary Street heart precinct, they are in the following terms (overall outcome 6): “a. Development in the District centre zone reinforces Boundary Street as the ‘heart’ of Spring Hill through support for a mix of uses and as a location for higher-order services. b. Development is of a height and scale that is subordinate to development in the City Centre expansion precinct. c. Development in the District centre zone or Mixed use zone is set back from, and provides a highly landscaped design response to adjoining sites in the Character residential zone or Low-medium density residential zone to minimise privacy and amenity impacts on residents. d. Development in the Mixed use zone supports existing community facilities in the area through the promotion of complementary uses, including Health care service and Short- term accommodation. e. Development promotes Boundary Street as a pedestrian friendly street through a combination of ground level activation, high quality street building and streetscape design, and welcoming front entrances.”

[99]The appellants contend there is non-compliance with overall outcome 6b. No other non-compliance is asserted with the precinct specific overall outcomes.

[100]Overall outcome 6b for the precinct requires development to be ‘subordinate to’ development in the City Centre expansion precinct. No party contended this planning objective is unsoundly based or overtaken by events. This is correct. The planning reflects an identifiable difference between the existing built form in Spring Hill and the CBD. For reasons that follow, an approval here will not change this position; the identifiable difference in built form between the precincts will remain.

[101]The term ‘subordinate’ in overall outcome 6b is not defined and, as a consequence, is to be given its plain meaning. In a built form context, and with overall outcome 4a of the SHNPC in mind, the term places a constraint on the three dimensional presentation of development (height and scale) in the Boundary Street heart precinct to achieve a transition to the south across precincts, bookended by the CBD. Overall outcome 6b does not however provide a clear test for how, or when, this will be achieved in the Boundary Street heart precinct despite items (1), (2) and (3) at

[95]. As a consequence, I accept the appellants’ submission that compliance with overall outcome 6b in any given case will turn on matters of fact, degree and impression: Ex.63, paras 5 and 7.

[102]When examining matters of fact and degree, I accept the appellants’ submission that the assessment is assisted by the expected height differential between the precincts, which is evident from Table 7.2.19.5.3.B; the table provides relevant context: Ex.63, paras 23 and 118. I also accept the assessment should take into account existing features of the locality, including topography: Ex.63, para 34.

[103]Table 7.2.19.5.3.A contains performance outcomes and acceptable outcomes for the SHNPC. Performance outcomes PO1 to PO7 apply to land in, inter alia, the Mixed use zone. The appellants contend there is non-compliance with performance outcomes PO1 and PO3: Ex.63, Annexure A, items 3 and 4. These provisions, including the associated acceptable outcomes are, in part, as follows: PO1 Development is of a height, scale and form that achieves the intended outcome for the precinct, improves the amenity of the neighbourhood AO1 Development complies with the number of storeys in Table 7.2.19.5.3.B – Maximum building height and maximum tower site plan area, contributes to a cohesive streetscape and built form character and is: a. consistent with anticipated density and assumed infrastructure demand; b. aligned to community expectations about the number of storeys to be built; c. proportionate and commensurate with the utility of the site area and frontage width; d. designed to avoid significant and undue adverse amenity impact to adjoining development; e. sited to enable existing and future buildings to be well separated from each other and to avoid affecting the potential development of an adjoining site. … Note––Development that is over- scaled for its site can result in an undesirable dominance of vehicle access, parking and manoeuvring areas that significantly reduce streetscape character and amenity. cover. … And: PO3 Development is of a scale and form that contributes to a cohesive streetscape and built form character, and: a. does not cause significant and undue adverse amenity impacts to adjoining properties, or prejudice their development; b. is sited and designed to enable existing and future buildings to be well separated from each other to allow for light penetration, air circulation between buildings, and the preservation of views, vistas AO3.1 Development ensures setbacks are in accordance with Table 7.2.19.5.3.D – Boundary setback requirements. AO3.2 Development has a maximum tower site cover in accordance with Table 7.2.19.5.3.B – Maximum building height and maximum tower site cover. … and resident privacy; c. protects the amenity of properties located in the Character residential zone or Low-medium density residential zone; d. respects the existing built form of properties identified in the Heritage overlay, Pre- 1911 building overlay, and the Commercial character building overlay.

[104]The proposed development does not comply with acceptable outcomes AO1 and AO3.1. An alternative performance based solution is advanced by Dibcorp. The terms of each performance outcome is, therefore, the focus of the assessment.

[105]The refusal cases advanced on behalf of the appellants did not assert non-compliance with performance outcomes PO1a or PO3c: Ex.63, Annexure A, items 3 and 4. These parts of the performance outcomes have therefore been struck through where they appear in the above extract.

[106]One disputed issue in the Burrell appeal requires reference to the Transport, access, parking and servicing code (TAPS code) and the planning scheme policy of the same name (TAPS policy). The provisions of the code and policy are traversed later in these reasons for judgment. For present purposes, it is sufficient to say that the traffic related provisions of the planning scheme raised in the Burrell appeal require, inter alia, development to provide access arrangements that are located, designed and controlled to minimise adverse impacts on the safety and operation of the road network, including the movement of pedestrians and cyclists. This can be demonstrated by, inter alia, compliance with the TAPS policy. It is contended, in reliance upon the evidence of Mr Holland, that the proposed access arrangement for service vehicles is unacceptable, but compliance can be achieved through the imposition of conditions. The conditions, if imposed, require a design change. Dibcorp resists the imposition of any such condition/s. Yi Shiu concept plans

[107]In support of the refusal case, Yi Shiu led evidence from two registered architects, namely Mr Richards and Mr Vojacek. The latter was retained to ‘prepare architectural plans and development yield diagrams for a code compliant multiple dwelling residential development which would represent the likely form of development’ on the Yi Shiu land: Ex.38, para 3.

[108]Mr Vojacek prepared a series of concept plans, massing studies and sun studies for the Yi Shiu land (the concept design). The concept design is said to be ‘indicative of the most likely form of a code compliant podium and tower development’: Ex.38, para 5. The development metrics controlling the design are contained in tabular form at paragraph 7 of Mr Vojacek’s statement (Ex.38). The metrics indicate that the concept design is intended to achieve compliance with acceptable outcomes in the SHNPC for building height, setbacks and tower site cover.

[109]The photomontages prepared for the appeal include images depicting the concept design. The photomontages, as is confirmed by the massing study, demonstrate the concept design is similar in height, but materially greater in scale, to the proposed development. The concept design is about twice the length of the proposed development in its north-south orientation.

[110]Consistent with the submissions made on behalf of Yi Shiu (Ex.63, para 53), I was conscious that the concept design: (1) does not have the benefit of a development approval; (2) has not been assessed against the entirety of the SHNPC; and (3) on Mr Richards’ evidence, requires refinement. For these reasons, caution was called for to ensure the concept design was not treated as a fait accompli. That said, the design is a guide to the redevelopment potential of the Yi Shiu land, assuming the acceptable outcomes in the SHNPC for building height, setbacks and tower site cover are applied. Put another way, the concept design is one that falls, in my view, within the range of reasonable expectations for new development in, and at the edge of, the Boundary Street heart precinct of the SHNPC on Leichhardt Street. The photomontages demonstrate that the three dimensional presentation of the concept design to Leichhardt Street is appreciably greater in its bulk and scale in comparison to the proposed development. The disputed issues

[111]The issues in dispute are defined by an order of the Court dated 19 February 2026. The order was made on the papers after the completion of the hearing. The order is consistent with the way the appeal was argued by all parties. The argument, particularly as it was advanced in written submissions, addresses the disputed issues by reference to the three propositions underlying the submitter appellants’ refusal case.

[112]After examining the disputed issues, the evidence and submissions of the parties, the matter having the greatest influence on the exercise of the planning discretion in both appeals is the assessment against the SHNPC. The appellants accept that an approval is difficult to resist in circumstances where compliance with this part of the planning scheme is demonstrated. It was common ground compliance with the SHNPC is demonstrated by complying with the overall outcomes and performance outcomes of the code.

[113]As a consequence, I will deal with an assessment against the provisions of the SHNPC first, followed by these questions: 1. Whether the proposed development is over-scaled for the site? 2. Whether the design of the access to Little Edward Street is unacceptable? 3. Whether, in the exercise of the discretion, the development application ought be approved subject to conditions? Assessment against the SHNPC

[114]The appellants allege the proposed development does not comply with six provisions of the SHNPC. The non-compliances relate to: (1) overall outcomes 3f, g and i; (2) overall outcome 6b; and (3) performance outcomes PO1 and PO3 (in part).

[115]The alleged non-compliances with the SHNPC are now considered in turn. Overall outcome 3f

[116]Overall outcome 3f is extracted at paragraph

[63]. The provision applies to the neighbourhood plan area as a whole and requires development to achieve two objectives. First, development is to reinforce the diverse architecture and urban form of Spring Hill. Second, development is to be responsive to each site’s specific shape, size, topography, context and setting.

[117]Dealing with the first of these objectives, there can be little doubt the existing built form and architectural character of Spring Hill is diverse: see

[11] to

[17]. The extent of diversity is evident in the visual aids before the Court, which demonstrate there is no homogenous built form outcome in Spring Hill. The existing built form varies in height, bulk, scale, age and appearance. Topography, road alignment and the subdivision pattern exaggerate and disguise differences in built form. Overall outcome 3f seeks development that reinforces the diversity of this architecture and urban form.

[118]I am satisfied the photomontages demonstrate the proposed development will strengthen the diversity of architecture and urban form of Spring Hill. The proposed development is an attractive modern built form, suitable in proportion and design for a visually prominent site. The attractiveness, and difference in appearance to existing development in the locality, is attributable, in large measure, to the design features discussed at

[25] to

[43]; the design features do not replicate existing development. The proposed development is also different to the design of approved built form discussed at

[19] to

[22]. The differences in built form (both existing and approved) are evident in height, bulk, orientation, articulation (in the horizontal and vertical planes) and building materials/colours. The extent of difference in building height is exaggerated at some viewing locations due to marked changes in topography and road alignment. This is best illustrated by the photomontages for viewpoints D and H.

[119]Overall outcome 3f also requires development to be responsive to a site’s shape, size, topography, context and setting. I am satisfied this has been achieved having regard, primarily, to the visual aids and evidence of Dr Robinson.

[120]The photomontages demonstrate the height, bulk and scale of the development is proportionate to the size and shape of the site. This is not due to any one feature of the proposal, but rather the product of: (1) a combination of design features that yield an attractive and proportionate modern building; and (2) the absence of unacceptable impacts that may, if present, be symptomatic of development that is disproportionate to, or discordant with, a site’s characteristics and setting: see

[164] to

[203].

[121]With respect to item (1), the design of the proposed development is discussed at

[25] to

[43]. Each of the features discussed therein has a part to play in the attractive presentation of the proposed development. Some features are worthy of particular attention in relation to an assessment against overall outcome 3f, namely: (a) the podium base, which will activate two street frontages with a green edge, provide separate pedestrian and vehicle access and facilitate a height transition from the tower to the pedestrian level; (b) the design strategies employed to manage and mitigate the bulk of the tower – this includes setting the tower back from common boundaries with adjoining land at distances similar to those provided in the approvals discussed at

[19] to

[22]; and (c) the retention and restoration of the heritage building fabric, which has been sensitively incorporated into the design.

[122]With respect to item (2), I have examined the potential impacts of the proposed development on the local area and immediate neighbours. For the local area, I am satisfied the proposed development will make an attractive contribution to Spring Hill: see

[25] to

[43] and

[118]. As to more immediate impacts, I am satisfied an approval would not give rise to unacceptable adverse impacts (in character and amenity terms) for adjoining land: see

[164] to

[203].

[123]A review of the photomontages makes good the appellants’ point that topography is an important part of the context and setting of Spring Hill. This is plain enough having regard to the fragmented skyline of built form in Spring Hill; the undulating nature of local topography (particularly from Leichhardt Street south down to the CBD); and the photomontages, which demonstrate the height and bulk of the proposed tower is visually prominent from a number of viewing locations, particularly to the south on Upper Edward Street. I am satisfied the photomontages demonstrate the proposed development is responsive to this local topography.

[124]The proposed development responds to local topography by the layering of built form components. The components provide a clear transition from the tower down to the podium and hotel façade. As between the tower and podium, the layering is achieved by a contrast in appearance. The former is setback from the edge of the podium. It is a stacked floor plate tower with a mixture of floor to ceiling glazing and a solid façade. The podium, on the other hand, is built to boundary and presents an active green edge to two street frontages, providing a cohesive human scale interface. The differentiation in appearance between the tower and podium, coupled with the setback of the tower, provides an attractive transition (in height and bulk) down to the street level, particularly at the intersection of Leichhardt Street and Little Edward Street. The design of the podium, and tower above, also appropriately respect the hotel façade rather than overwhelm it. This outcome has been achieved by the design attributes discussed at

[27] and

[29], taken in combination with the cantilevered position of the new podium above the hotel.

[125]Mr Olsson was called by the appellants to assist the Court with visual amenity considerations. He concluded that the proposed development would not reinforce the diverse urban form and architecture that characterises Spring Hill by reason of its height. The reasons Mr Olsson give for this conclusion are to be found in the Visual amenity and landscape architecture joint report. In summary terms, the reasons can be reduced to the following five propositions, namely: (a) the proposed 17 storey tower is the tallest in the ‘Local Area’ (the relevant area is identified in Figure 11 of the Visual amenity and landscape architecture joint report); (b) the height of the proposed development does not provide a consistent and cohesive built form in the streetscape; (c) the 6 storey building on the Burrell site, for which the proposal will be overbearing, is unlikely to be developed in the near future due to its narrow width and little opportunity for amalgamation; (d) the proposal is excessively bulky when juxtaposed with the concept design for the Yi Shiu site; and (e) the proposal has an architectural character which is not dissimilar to mixed use multi-dwelling buildings in the ‘Local Area’ (more of the same) because it has applied concrete finishes, glazed balustrades and projecting floor slabs.

[126]I do not accept Mr Olsson’s opinion that the proposed development, by reason of its height, will not reinforce the diverse architecture and urban form of Spring Hill. This is so for a number of reasons. Subparagraph (a) is factually wrong. The proposed development is shorter, in metres, than the approved development at 25 Fortescue Street and 157 Leichhardt Street. Both sites are located in the ‘Local Area’ defined in the joint report to which Mr Olsson contributed. Subparagraph (b) is not, in my view, made good by the photomontages: see

[26] to

[31],

[43] and

[124]. As to subparagraph (c), while the proposed development is taller than the existing building on the Burrell land, and the Burrell land may not be redeveloped, neither point takes the matter very far. The photomontages, read with proposed conditions of approval, do not indicate the resulting visual relationship between the proposed development and the building on the Burrell land is unacceptable in the context of Spring Hill. This is confirmed by the building-to-building relationship approved at the interface of the Burrell building with approved development at 25 Fortescue Street. It is also confirmed by the acceptable outcome for building height in the SHNPC, which admits of the prospect that low (3 storeys) to mid rise development may share an interface with high rise development (15 storeys with no limit prescribed in metres) in the same precinct. Subparagraph (d) is not made good by the photomontages. Nor is it made good by the massing study attached to Mr Vojacek’s statement. The photomontages and massing study indicate the three dimensions of the proposed development, and the resulting bulk and scale: (1) are proportionate to the size and shape of the site; and (2) have been successfully mitigated by the design elements identified at

[37] to

[40]. Subparagraph (e) is correct as a statement of fact, but does little, in and of itself, to advance the assessment against overall outcome 3f. This is because, while the proposed development has similarities that can be identified with existing development, it also has clear differences (in terms of height, scale, appearance, age and architectural typology). It is the combination of the similarities and differences that will reinforce the diversity of architecture and urban form in Spring Hill.

[127]The appellants’ written submissions contend there is non-compliance with overall outcome 3f because the proposed development: (a) does not reinforce the diverse architecture and urban form that sets Spring Hill apart from other parts of Brisbane (Ex.63, para 113) – it is more of the same (Ex.63, para 114); (b) ignores the site’s specific shape, size, topography, context and setting – it sits on top of one of Spring Hill’s ridgelines, at a visible corner site in the Boundary Street heart precinct (Ex.63, para 113); and (c) is over-scaled (Ex.63, para 114).

[128]For the reasons given above, I do not accept the submissions summarised at subparagraphs (a) or (b). As to subparagraph (c), the simplicity of the summary belies the multi-faceted nature of this allegation. I deal with the over-scaling point at

[253] to

[255]. For the reasons set out therein, I am satisfied the proposed development is not over-scaled.

[129]I am satisfied the proposed development complies with overall outcome 3f of the SHNPC. Overall outcome 3g

[130]Overall outcome 3g applies to the neighbourhood plan area as a whole. The provision is set out at

[63].

[131]Overall outcome 3g requires development to achieve two objectives. First, development is to be designed to take advantage of Brisbane’s climate and deliver high quality, subtropical architecture. Second, the design of buildings, the public realm and landscaped spaces are to be open, engaging and green, with shaded spaces and opportunities to interact with the street, and contribute to Brisbane’s identity and lifestyle.

[132]The appellants submit there is non-compliance with overall outcome 3g because the proposed development does not deliver high quality, subtropical architecture. In support of this contention, it was further submitted the photomontages depict a repetitive, block-like built form, the height and scale of which is not effectively mitigated using architectural strategies and landscaping. The landscaping is said to be too minimal. It is also pointed out that the landscaping regimen does not include deep planting.

[133]Dr Robinson and Mr Richards examined the design of the proposal to assess its response to the subtropical climate of Brisbane. The appellants rely upon the evidence of Mr Richards to establish the response is unacceptable and does not comply with the planning scheme. Mr Richards’ evidence in this regard is brief (Ex.9, Burrell appeal, para 129): “129. The ‘Dibcorp site’ development has elements of appropriate subtropical design predominantly on the podium. The tower however, is not a strong example of subtropical design. The building has full height glazed facades with the same expression on each facade with little or no sun shading. The 300mm slab overhang contributes little to subtropical design especially on the east and west facades where full height vertical screening is appropriate. The balconies are completely recessed into the building facade. They do not provide any articulation or modelling to the facade for casting shadows that would animate the facade expression as the sun passes through the sky during the day. There are no screens or trellises to provide additional sun screening and privacy on these balconies.”

[134]Mr Richards’ limited his criticism to the tower component of the proposed development. The criticism is expressed in mild terms. He said this element of the proposed development is not a ‘strong example of subtropical design’. Evidence of this kind falls short of establishing that the design of the proposal is unacceptable in subtropical design terms. Nor does it establish non-compliance with overall outcome 3g.

[135]Further, I am far from persuaded that Mr Richards’ evidence provides a satisfactory basis, in any event, to arrive at a conclusion about the effectiveness, or otherwise, of the subtropical design response here. In simple terms, the evidence makes plain that the criticism is directed towards the negative attributes of the design alone. The evidence does not allow a balance to be struck with the positive elements, such as those in the design of the podium that Mr Richards accepts are appropriate. The positive elements of the design have not been identified with any particularity.

[136]The effectiveness of the subtropical design response will, in my view, be the product of its parts, both positive and negative. All parts of the design need to be examined and balanced for an overall impression. To look only at the negatives takes too narrow a view; it lacks balance. The lack of balance is problematic once it is appreciated that the subject matter being examined: (1) is not capable of empirical analysis; (2) is assessed by reference to broad motherhood statements rather than prescriptive standards; and (3) is ultimately a matter about which reasonable minds may, and do, differ.

[137]In any event, for reasons that follow, I am satisfied Mr Richards’ criticisms of the design should not be accepted or, alternatively, do not dimmish the merit of the design in subtropical terms. The criticisms that found his conclusion can be summarised as: (1) a lack of sun shading on the tower; (2) the use of recessed balconies in the tower; and (3) an absence of shading/privacy screens.

[138]With respect to (1), an examination of the photomontages, together with Dr Robinson’s evidence, confirms the criticism about the 300mm slab overhang, and the extent to which it will provide meaningful shade, is not without force. More shade could be provided to the façade. It does not however follow that the development fails to exhibit a subtropical design response because ‘more’ shade could be provided to the façade. The shade response to the façade is appropriate having regard to Dr Robinson’s evidence, which I accept and is set out below in detail.

[139]With respect to (2), I am satisfied the photomontages illustrate, contrary to Mr Richards’ view, that the balconies will provide articulation and modelling to the façade. The articulation and animation of shade will occur due to the recessed design of the balconies. The sun and shadow will, by reason of the recess, touch different parts of the façade on different alignments at different times.

[140]With respect to (3), it is correct to say that the design does not include screens or trellises to protect recessed balconies from the sun and to provide privacy. The evidence does not however suggest screening of this kind is required. To the contrary, the screens, if adopted, would adversely impact on an adopted design strategy to manage the bulk of the building. The strategy was described by Mr Curtis as ‘feathering’. This part of the design enables the building to visually ‘erode’ at the edges where recessed balconies are provided. Screening of balconies would have the opposite effect. The screening would impede the ‘feathering’ effect and increase the visual bulk of the building at its edges.

[141]In contrast to Mr Richards, Dr Robinson adopted a fulsome approach to the assessment of subtropical architecture. With overall outcome 3i and performance outcome PO6 of the SHNPC in mind, he examined the design response from two perspectives: (1) in light of existing built form character and lifestyle; and (2) in terms of its response to user comfort requirements.

[142]The first part of the assessment involved an examination of the existing built form and streetscape character of Spring hill. This exercise led Dr Robinson to conclude there is a generally poor and unattractive response to Brisbane’s subtropical climate in Spring Hill. I accept this evidence. It is made good having regard to the visual aids which depict: (1) the local streetscapes, which do not exhibit a cohesive landscaping strategy – landscaping and awnings are rare; (2) apartment buildings that have a distinct lack of greenery and planters to soften built form; and (3) retail and office buildings, which lack greenery, are not permeable, provide minimal shade and lack opportunities to ‘open up’ façades.

[143]Dr Robinson’s assessment of existing built form and streetscape character, taken together with the planning scheme provisions referred to above, led him to reach this conclusion: an increasingly subtropical character and identity for Spring hill is envisaged by the planning scheme. He regarded the proposed development as an acceptable response to this planning intention. Paragraph 137 of the Architecture joint report (Ex.9, Burrell appeal) records Dr Robinson’s reasons for this conclusion, which is in the following terms: “137. Generally I find the proposal’s character response to Brisbane’s subtropical climate to be acceptable. In my opinion the proposal will drive creation of the type of subtropical character and identity the planning scheme is envisaging for Spring Hill at overall outcome 3(i) of the SHNP. Key factors that support this opinion include: i. The ground level public realm entries and courtyards are open, engaging, green and shaded. ii. The proposal’s podium levels are lined at the street interface with landscaped planters and shaded recessed colonnades and balconies. iii. New subtropical landscaping has been provided to the Leichhardt Street and Little Edward Street frontages including street trees and ground cover planting in the verge. Combined with the building’s street building’s planters, this establishes the desired landscaped, shaded, subtropical streetscape character and will become part of a larger cohesive urban pattern as other nearby sites are developed. iv. The apartments open up to the elements, natural daylight and natural ventilation with balconies and operable doors and windows. v. A horizontal sunshade wraps around each level to provide partial shading to glazing. vi. Balcony spaces are sized to permit outdoor dining and recreation. vii. The rooftop will be landscaped, open, engaging, green, shaded and will provide building occupants with the communal outdoor lifestyle choice.”

[144]A review of the proposed architectural and landscape plans, along with the photomontages, makes good Dr Robinson’s opinion. I accept his evidence. Taken in combination, the above measures represent an acceptable climate design response and, in the context of existing built form and streetscape character, will make a positive step towards achieving a new subtropical design character in Spring Hill.

[145]For completeness, I am satisfied the weight to be given to this evidence is not diminished by the criticism directed at subparagraph v above by Mr Richards. The 300mm sunhood is a feature of the design Dr Robinson described as minimal. Dr Robinson said he would have provided more sun shading if he was the project architect. It does not however follow from this concession that subparagraph v is overstated or wrong. The subparagraph correctly speaks of ‘partial shading’. I am satisfied the 300mm sunhood will provide partial shading to the façade. This is relevant to the assessment of the design response to subtropical climate considerations.

[146]The second perspective from which Dr Robinson assessed the subtropical design response was user comfort. By reference to performance outcome PO6 of the SHNPC, this topic involves consideration of building orientation, ventilation, cross-ventilation, heating, cooling and light. The evidence about these matters is detailed. It is unnecessary to set it out in full. It is sufficient to say that I am satisfied the evidence directed to these matters comfortably demonstrates the design has sought to minimise the need for mechanical heating, cooling and lighting in Brisbane’s climate. This is evident from a review of the proposed plans, read together with subparagraphs iv to vii of Dr Robinson’s evidence above. I note Dr Robinson’s opinions about these matters were not the subject of disagreement with Mr Richards.

[147]Overall outcome 3g requires development to be designed to take advantage of Brisbane’s climate and deliver high quality, subtropical architecture. I am satisfied, in light of the above, that compliance has been demonstrated with this part of overall outcome 3g.

[148]Turning to the second part of overall outcome 3g, I am satisfied the proposed development is designed to be open, engaging and green with shaded spaces and opportunities to interact with the street. This is evident at the podium levels of the development depicted in the photomontages, read together with the evidence of Dr Robinson and Mr Brooksby. The appellants’ contend otherwise, pointing to a lack of landscaping on-site, in particular, a lack of deep planting.

[149]The importance ascribed to the absence of deep planting is anchored in provisions of the Centre or mixed use code and the Multiple dwelling code. These codes call for deep planting and landscaping to, inter alia, establish large tropical shade trees. Deep planting is distinguished from other types of planting by reason that it is located directly over natural ground (not basements or structures) and can support: (1) subtropical planting; and (2) the long-term growth of large subtropical shade trees: Ex.11, Yi Shiu appeal, para 490. An acceptable outcome in the planning scheme related to deep planting includes a dimensional criterion of 4 metres x 4 metres. This is expressed as a minimum dimension for deep planting.

[150]The proposed development does not meet the minimum dimension for deep planting. Further, the proposed development, in its present form, cannot be conditioned to make provision for deep planting and large subtropical shade trees.

[151]While it can be accepted that the provision made for deep planting and large shade trees in the proposal is a matter to which consideration is to be given in the assessment of the proposed development, I do not regard the absence of such as indicia of overdevelopment in this case.

[152]The starting point in this regard is the SHNPC. This document, as is conceded by all, represents the most locally focused and fine grained planning for the land and Spring Hill. I was not directed to any provision that requires deep planting to be provided at ground level and on-site. This is perhaps unsurprising given the SHNPC promotes a particular built form outcome in the Mixed use zone that is likely to render the provision of deep planting at street level difficult, if not, unrealistic. The built form outcome to which I refer is a ‘street building’, referred to in performance outcome PO4 and Figure c of the SHNPC. Reference to performance outcome PO4 and Figure c reveals the SHNPC promotes, on the site, a façade that is built to boundary and treated to, inter alia, address and activate the street. This can be achieved through, inter alia, a street building built to boundary that has vertical landscaping and shade structures. In combination with building articulation, the vertical landscape and shade structure provide shade and shelter for pedestrians on the street (AO4.3c).

[153]Against this background, it is my view the landscaping of importance for the proposal is that contributing to the streetscape experience. This comprises the landscaping in the verge, on ground level and in podium level planters. Mr Brooksby, who is a landscape architect, confirmed the photomontages illustrate the proposed landscaping treatments in these locations. The photomontages demonstrate the proposed landscaping, in conjunction with the built form, will be engaging and contribute to a cohesive streetscape and subtropical character.

[154]Mr Brooksby, who is a landscape architect, was not concerned about the absence of deep planting. As I understood his evidence, this was so for three reasons, which I accept.

[155]First, Mr Brooksby accepted the proposed planting regime did not provide for ‘deep planting’ but included planting that achieves a similar outcome, namely it has: (1) direct access to natural ground; and (2) an absence of subsurface structural encumbrances: Ex.11, Yi Shiu appeal, para 491. To make good this point, Mr Brooksby pointed to landscape treatments at the ground level, which include multiple in-ground planting beds unencumbered by basement structures: Ex.11, Yi Shiu appeal, para 492. The planting beds to which Mr Brooksby referred are (Ex.11, Yi Shiu appeal, paras 493 and 495): (1) the in-ground planting beds on natural ground, located either side of the Little Edward Street driveway; and (2) the in-ground planting beds adjacent to the pedestrian entry along Leichhardt Street, and an area to the east that accommodates a medium shade tree.

[156]Second, Mr Brooksby said the primary purpose of deep planting, and the dimensional criterion stated in an acceptable outcome, is to enable the establishment of large subtropical shade trees. With this purpose in mind, Mr Brooksby observed: “…I consider the inclusion of large subtropical shade trees to be unsuitable, having regard to the constrained urban setting, the application of CPTED principles, and the planning intent to deliver a visually open, human-scaled and interaction-focused streetscape interface.”

[157]I accept this point particularly in light of what I have observed at

[152].

[158]Third, Mr Brooksby concluded that the absence of deep planting and large subtropical shade trees was not unacceptable because the configuration of the landscaping proposed at pedestrian interfaces (and building edges) will still contribute to streetscape character and support a subtropical landscape outcome: Ex.11, Yi Shiu appeal, para 498. The specific landscaping to which reference was made is located at the ground floor, level 1 and level 2, being the most publicly visible and interface- driven parts of the proposal. The photomontages make good on this evidence.

[159]I am satisfied compliance has been demonstrated with overall outcome 3g. Overall outcome 3i

[160]Overall outcome 3i applies to the neighbourhood plan area as a whole. The extract of the provision is at

[63].

[161]Overall outcome 3i has two objectives. The provision calls for slender towers in, inter alia, the Mixed use zone that are: (a) sited and designed to maintain the openness of street vistas; and (b) sited and designed with adequate spacing between buildings to allow for light penetration, air circulation, views and vistas, and privacy, particularly for residential towers.

[162]In the first instance, the appellants submit the photomontages do not support a finding that the proposed tower is slender from most viewpoints. Specific reference in this regard was made to the photomontages at pp.25, 26, 37 and 38 of exhibit 6. A review of these photomontages makes good this submission to the extent it is based solely on matters of visual impression. The tower, while appreciably taller than it is wide, is not slender in appearance from most viewpoints.

[163]For reasons given at

[79] and

[80], this is not the end of the matter. It is necessary to go on and examine the balance of overall outcome 3i, in particular, the two parts set out above, which comprise an expression of the planning purpose that sits behind the desire for ‘slender towers’. This is particularly so here given the acceptable outcomes in the SHNPC for building height and setbacks yield a building envelope less slender in appearance than the proposed development: see

[78].

[164]Overall outcome 3i requires development to maintain the openness of street vistas. I was not referred to any definition of the term ‘street vista’. It is to be given its plain meaning. In the context of the SHNPC, the phrase captures, in my view, a long view following the alignment of the road. The area of focus is the space created in the street between the built form, which provides an edge on either side of the road. Maintaining a sense of openness requires development, in my view, not to crowd or cramp the space between the built form edges.

[165]The photomontages provide an excellent tool for assessing impacts on the street vista and the associated sense of space and openness. For this particular exercise, the photomontage viewpoints can be divided into three groups: (1) viewpoints of the Leichhardt Street vista (VPA, B and D); (2) viewpoints of the Little Edward Street vista (VPC2 and G); and (3) viewpoints of the Upper Edward Street vista (VPE and H).

[166]Leichhardt Street is not straight. The road reserve meanders as it follows a ridge line (west to north-east). This has an impact on the sense of openness and space of the Leichhardt Street vista. The photomontages for viewpoints A, B and D vividly illustrate how the meandering road corridor results in built form encroaching into the openness and space of the ‘street vista’. The meandering alignment of the road causes the vista to be punctuated, to varying degrees, by existing built form along its edges. The street vista depicted in viewpoints B and D is particularly overcrowded with existing built form, reducing the sense of space and openness of the streetscape.

[167]I am satisfied the photomontages for viewpoints A, B and D do not suggest the proposed development will crowd or cramp the Leichhardt Street ‘street vista’. The openness and space in that vista is already encroached upon by existing development. The proposed development will not alter this outcome in any adverse way.

[168]I would add to the above that the proposed development, in my view, will improve the street vista when seen from a position to the east of the intersection at Leichhardt Street/Little Edward Street/Upper Edward Street. The improvement is apparent in the photomontage for viewpoint A. In this photomontage, the sense of openness of the street vista is maintained by the clear setbacks provided in the design from the hotel to the podium to the tower. The setback from the street vista as the built form increases in height is readily apparent. So too is the retained and restored façade of the heritage building fabric. These elements of the design, in combination, make for an attractive addition to this part of the streetscape. The design features provide a clear visual demarcation with the tower above, which has been designed, successfully, to include measures that mitigate its height, bulk and scale.

[169]That the proposed development will not adversely impact on the Leichhardt Street vista is confirmed, in my view, by the photomontages for viewpoints A and D depicting the built form of the Yi Shiu concept plan. The concept plan, which is said to be code compliant in terms of building height and setback, will have a greater impact on the openness of the Leichhardt Street ‘street vista’ than the proposed development, particularly from viewpoints A and D. In these viewpoints, the built form appears to extend, to a relatively small degree, further into the street vista than the proposed development. This distinction in setback from the street vista assists, to some degree, in mitigating a visual merging of the two built forms. Visual merging will be further mitigated when the final design for the Yi Shiu site is refined. Refinements to building colours, layout and the like will assist in drawing a greater distinction between the façade of the proposed development and the Yi Shiu development.

[170]The alignment of Little Edward Street is, for all intents and purposes, straight in a north-south direction. The straight road reserve means the photomontages for viewpoints C2 and G permit a clear distinction to be drawn between the street and the existing built form edge. A sense of space and openness is apparent in the street vista. So too, at street level, is the presence of built to boundary development and mature street trees.

[171]I am satisfied the photomontages for viewpoints C2 and G demonstrate the proposed development will maintain the openness of the street vista along Little Edward Street. The built form will not intrude or encroach on the sense of openness of that vista. Rather, in my view, the proposed development will improve the openness and attractiveness of the vista. The attractiveness of the street vista is improved by the combination of design features evident in the podium and tower above. The former, which integrates the heritage building fabric, will provide an activated green edge at pedestrian level. This is distinct from the tower above, which is set back from the edge of the podium. The design strategies employed to mitigate the height, bulk and scale of the tower are readily identifiable when examined in the viewpoints for the Little Edward Street ‘street vista’.

[172]That the proposed development will not adversely impact on the openness of the Little Edward Street vista, is confirmed particularly by the photomontage for viewpoint G. In that photomontage, the approved but not yet constructed built form at 25 Fortescue Street is obvious. The bulk, height and scale of the approved development is significant. The proposed development, in conjunction with the development at 25 Fortescue Street, maintains the openness of the ‘street vista’. This is achieved by a combination of their siting (i.e. the tower components are setback from Little Edward Street) and overall design (i.e. vertical and horizontal articulation, modulation and provision of an attractive pedestrian environment at ground level).

[173]At first blush, the problematic viewpoints for the proposed development are viewpoints E and G. They both depict the Upper Edward Street vista. The proposed tower component is visually prominent in each image. The built form appears to obscure, or substantially close, the northern end of the vista. The openness of the vista does not appear to be maintained by the proposed development. This is not unacceptable once the following matters are appreciated.

[174]First, there is a material change in road alignment at the intersection of Upper Edward Street/Leichhardt Street/Little Edward Street. Upper Edward Street is not aligned north-south like Little Edward Street. Aerial imagery shows that Little Edward Street doglegs at the key intersection to the north-east away from the alignment of Upper Edward Street. The resulting alignment is such that the northern view along Upper Edward Street terminates in a diagonal view across the southern and eastern faces of the site. This diagonal view into the site and through the key intersection contributes significantly to an impression that the openness of the street vista will not be maintained by the proposed development. The angle of the view to the site is such that the vista was always at risk of closure by the introduction of built form on the site, which is anticipated by the SHNPC.

[175]Second, the Upper Edward Street vista will, in any event, be materially narrowed by existing and approved development. This is confirmed by the photomontage for viewpoint H. In that image, the building on the Burrell land can be seen terminating the street vista behind the heritage façade. The vista is also terminated to an extent by the approved development to the north of the site on Little Edward Street and Fortescue Street.

[176]Third, the SHNPC promotes high rise development on the site and adjoining land to the north in the Boundary Street heart precinct. Given that amalgamation is incentivised in that precinct, it is reasonable to expect development of at least 10 storeys on the land. Development of that height, for which there is no prescribed metric in metres, will, like the proposed development, materially reduce the openness of the street vista looking north along Upper Edward Street.

[177]The three matters set out above indicate that the issue to be examined, in terms of the openness of the Upper Edward Street vista, is not binary. The point involves matters of degree. In simple terms, existing, approved and intended development, given the alignment of the road network, will all have the appearance of narrowing the street vista. The proposed development is no different. It will, if approved, narrow the street vista. The issue is whether the extent to which the proposed development contributes to this is unacceptable. In my view, the impact will not be unacceptable.

[178]The photomontages, in my view, do not suggest the extent to which the proposed development will narrow the street vista on Upper Edward Street is unacceptable. The site, given the road alignment and intended form of development, is always going to be visually prominent and encroach into the vista. That prominence is not due to a lack of setback along Leichhardt Street or Little Edward Street. Nor is it due to the height of the built form. Built form of similar height at 25 Fortescue Street will, as the photomontages suggest, significantly narrow the Upper Edward Street vista without the proposed development. The proposed development will not further narrow that vista in an unacceptable way.

[179]I am satisfied the proposed development has been sited and designed to maintain the openness of street vistas.

[180]Overall outcome 3i requires consideration to be given to the spacing between the proposed development and nearby buildings. Building to building spacing or separation is examined to ensure it is sufficient to allow light penetration, air circulation, views and vistas and privacy.

[181]The Leichhardt Street and Little Edward Street road reserves provide a generous separation distance between the site and land to the south and east. The separation distance is such that the proposed development, even though it is located towards the south-eastern corner of the site, is well removed from land to the south and east to avoid adverse impacts on light penetration, air circulation, views and vistas and privacy. This is consistent with Dr Robinson’s opinion with which Mr Richards did not disagree. I accept Dr Robinson’s evidence in this regard.

[182]The separation between the proposed development and adjoining land to the north and west requires more detailed consideration. The extent of physical separation provided is depicted in the photomontages for viewpoints B, C1, C2, I1 and I2. The setback provided at level 3 of the tower to the northern and western boundaries is 4.3 metres and 4.25 metres respectively. This can be compared to the acceptable solution provided for side and rear setbacks in the SHNPC, which is 6 metres.

[183]I am satisfied the setback provided to the northern boundary allows for acceptable light penetration, air circulation, views and vistas and privacy. This is so whether the existing development or a potential redevelopment is considered for the land to the north.

[184]With respect to existing development, the land to the north is improved with a 6 storey brick faced commercial office building. The southern façade is built to boundary (0 metre setback) for its full length, save for 3.5 metres at its eastern end, and is punctuated with 15 small windows on levels 1 to 5. The windows provide visual access for areas appearing to be dedicated to services and amenities. As to the floor plates, they are open plan for each level, positioned on the northern side of the land, facing away from the site. Horizontal strip windows on the northern and eastern façades, which face away from the site, provide access to daylight. The roof has no occupied deck.

[185]I am satisfied the separation provided between the proposed tower and the existing Burrell building, which is provided almost solely on the site due to the latter being built to boundary, will not adversely impact on light penetration, air circulation, views and vistas and privacy. The setback provided allows for light penetration and air circulation. This is not only due to the separation distance provided but because of the orientation of the Burrell Building. As discussed above, access to daylight, breezes, views, vistas and matters of privacy arises as issues for the northern and eastern façades of the proposed development. The Burrell building is not orientated to face the site. Further, a review of the proposed plans of development reveal that the northern facing units in the proposed development are orientated east-west, with narrow vertical windows and a side balcony facing the Burell building. Any northward view will have a narrow field of focus and, in many cases, look over the Burrell building rather than at its southern façade.

[186]For completeness, it can be observed that one of the initial concerns expressed by Mr Olsson and Mr Richards was about the design of the interface between the proposed development and the Burrell building. By the end of the hearing, this ceased to be a matter warranting refusal; Dibcorp indicated it would accept conditions recommended by Dr McGowan and Dr Robinson about the treatment of the interface. Compliance with the conditions will address the concerns raised by Mr Olsson and Mr Richards in this regard. There is, as a consequence, no need to dwell further upon this interface.

[187]Dr Robinson considered the potential impact of the proposed development on the Burrell land on the premise it was redeveloped for a high rise residential building between 9 and 15 storeys, depending on the amalgamation of sites. It was also assumed by Dr Robinson that: (1) the configuration of the redevelopment would be similar to the 25 Fortescue Street approval, orientated east-west to maximise outlook, access to light and breezes; (2) the side boundaries (north and south) of the redevelopment are treated as secondary frontages, with setbacks similar to the 25 Fortescue Street approval, ranging from 4 to 4.45 metres (average of 4 metres). I accept this evidence. It was not challenged by Mr Richards.

[188]There are a number of features of any future redevelopment of the Burrell land, taken in conjunction with the proposed development, that indicate building-to-building separation will be sufficient to comply with overall outcome 3i. This is so for two reasons. First. the extent of physical separation that can be achieved is in the order of 8.5 metres, assuming 4 to 4.5 metres either side of the common boundary. Separation of this distance will allow adequate sun and day light penetration. It will also allow for breezes and cross-ventilation. Second, views, vistas and privacy will be preserved by reason of the orientation of any redevelopment on the Burrell land. It will look towards the east and west, rather than the north or south. Privacy impacts at the interface at the common boundary are mitigated by the design of the proposed development. The northern apartments are orientated east-west and have only narrow openings and a small balcony facing north. The field of view to the north is intentionally narrow.

[189]I am satisfied the setback provided to the Yi Shiu land (western boundary) allows for acceptable light penetration, air circulation, views and vistas and privacy. This is so whether the existing development or potential redevelopment is considered.

[190]Land adjoining the site to the west is improved with a 3 storey commercial building, and a commercially operated basement carpark. The commercial building is built to boundary along its northern and eastern façades. Setbacks are provided to Leichhardt Street and Fortescue Street. The setback to Leichhardt Street is approximately 18 metres and accommodates an external courtyard area at ground level, which is used as a recreation space by students attending an industry college. The floor plates of the building are large. Access to natural daylight and ventilation is obtained from windows facing Leichhardt Street and Fortescue Street. The building has no occupiable deck. The external walls facing the site have no windows.

[191]The proposed development will not impede access to daylight, air circulation, views and vistas and privacy for the existing building on the Yi Shiu land. This is due to the design of the existing building. It turns its back to the site; there is no ability to look from the proposal into the existing site. That the existing building turns its back to the site is also significant in terms of views, access to sunlight and ventilation. Access is achieved to sunlight, daylight, ventilation and views via the south and west (i.e. from the opposite direction of the site).

[192]It was pointed out on behalf of Yi Shiu that the proposed development will have adverse impacts, in terms of shadowing and overlooking, on the recreation space used by students attending the industry college. The principal of the college was called to give evidence about this. He explained the importance of the courtyard area for the students’ well-being and the need to protect their privacy, particularly from development overlooking the external courtyard.

[193]It can be readily accepted that the well-being of students and the protection of their privacy is a matter of considerable import for the college. The evidence here does not however suggest the concern will be realised to any unacceptable degree. I am satisfied this is the case having regard to Dr Robinson’s evidence. His evidence on this point comprises two parts. First, Dr Robinson correctly pointed out what may be regarded as obvious; the courtyard is located within an increasingly urbanised environment. The planning documents promote an urbanised environment. Taller buildings are reasonably expected. Where development of this kind is promoted, like here, it is unreasonable to expect the student courtyard would remain free from overshadowing, overlooking and overbearing development. The second part of the evidence comprises a detailed review of impacts at Figures NR-SK-1 to 4 of the Architecture joint report prepared for the Yi Shiu appeal (Ex.9).

[194]The four figures referred to above make good the following evidence of Dr Robinson (Ex.9, p.30): “…I have prepared diagrams NR-SK-1 through NR-SK-4 at Appendix B of this AJER to explore any potential amenity impacts by the proposal upon this courtyard. With reference to those diagrams, I note that: i. The courtyard is already overshadowed at times by the Yi Shiu building from the North – this is seen in diagram NR-SK-1. In an urban environment, where the courtyard already sits at ground level to the south of multi storey built form, the proposed building is unlikely to provide significant additional adverse shadowing compared to a code compliant height building with acceptable outcome setbacks. ii. The courtyard already has what appear to be small roof structures installed over some of the courtyard’s picnic tables, which will provide both weather protection and a degree of privacy. iii. The courtyard will most likely be used during business hours, the time when most residents are more likely to be absent from their apartments. iv. The proposal’s podium which will have a built to boundary adjacency to the courtyard is 2 storeys high where adjacent to the courtyard – equivalent to the existing Hotel extension buildings that until recently occupied the subject site… v. At the lowest level of the tower, the apartment on the south-west corner of the proposal is orientated to face Leichhardt Street and will have negligible overlooking potential, from within the apartments, due to minimal windows and the effects of parallax…Partial views into the courtyard from the external terrace of that apartment, will be possible…but the terrace is less likely to be occupied during business hours when the Yi Shiu site’s courtyard is in use, and I envisage any moments of overlooking upon the courtyard to be temporary and not sustained; an outcome reasonable in an inner city urban environment. vi. The proposed apartment (in the proposal) one step back from the Leichhardt Street frontage has minimal adjacency to the courtyard, it will largely be shielded behind the bulk of the Yi Shiu building…I don’t anticipate any unreasonable or sustained overlooking potential. vii. At higher levels of the proposal tower, the same apartment orientations persist, but the physical separation from the courtyard also increases…The separating powers of distance and parallax will increase.”

[195]The overlooking concern raised on behalf of Yi Shiu was directed to a concern that occupants of two or three units in the proposed development, located at podium level, would peer over the edge of the balcony (i.e. the edge of the podium) into the courtyard. This concern is a legitimate one. An impact of this kind is fairly regarded as adverse in privacy terms. It does not however warrant refusal. Nor does it necessarily establish non-compliance with overall outcome 3i. Relevant planning context reveals that reasonable expectations as to future development on the site ought include the prospect of a ‘street building’, which comprises a podium and a setback tower form above it. Where development of this kind is to be anticipated, it should be reasonably expected that an impact may include overlooking from the edge of a podium into adjoining land, such as has been raised here. Further, for reasons given by Dr Robinson, that impact is not unacceptable in the circumstances.

[196]Dr McGowan suggested that conditions could, but need not necessarily, be imposed to mitigate the overlooking impact into the courtyard. The conditions he had in mind require the continuation of containerised planting from the southern façade along the western edge of the podium. The extended planters create a physical buffer between the edge of the balcony and the edge of the podium, limiting access to the latter and, in turn, reducing overlooking into the courtyard. Dibcorp did not embrace conditions of this kind. It submitted they were unnecessary. I agree. Conditions of the kind recommended by Dr McGowan are unnecessary to demonstrate compliance with overall outcome 3i.

[197]The sufficiency of the separation provided, by way of setback, to the western boundary was examined by Dr Robinson assuming the Yi Shiu land was redeveloped in accordance with the concept plans prepared by Mr Vojacek.

[198]Dr Robinson carried out a detailed shadow and daylight analysis, which is diagrammatically represented in Figures NR-SK-6 to NR-SK-26 of the Architecture joint report for the Yi Shiu appeal (Ex.9). I have examined the figures in conjunction with the explanatory text. The figures and text make good Dr Robinson’s overall conclusion that the proposed development would cause only a negligible, to minor, impact on direct sunlight access for the apartments in the Yi Shiu concept plans. This is due to the orientation of the concept design prepared by Mr Vojacek. The building’s exposed north-eastern aspect provides good sunlight access. Any change to the proposed development’s setback or height would provide limited to no additional benefit.

[199]Dr Robinson also examined the potential for adverse impacts on the Yi Shiu concept design in terms of views, privacy and breezes. Again, Dr Robinson’s analysis was represented diagrammatically in Figure NR-SK-5 of the Architecture joint report for the Yi Shiu appeal (Ex.9). The figure, read with the accompanying text, makes good the following observations about the potential for adverse impacts on the Yi Shiu concept design: “…My review has revealed the following: i. The 3 x 2 bed units contained in the projection (which I have noted as apartments C, D and E on diagram NR-SK-5 at Appendix C), will enjoy excellent privacy and open long distance eastern and north-eastern views… ii. The 1 bed unit in the middle of the Yi Shiu concept floor plan (which I have referred to as Apartment B on diagram NR-SK- 05) will enjoy primary long distance north-eastern views diagonally past the north-western corner of the proposal building. The edge transparency created by the proposal’s recessed corner balconies, will widen the sense of openness, view line and vista for the one bed apartment… iii. At the southern end of the Yi Shiu concept building the 3 bed unit (which I have referred to as Apartment A…) is orientated to face south, with its primary living areas, primary (master) bedroom and balcony enjoying excellent privacy and open long distance views across Leichhardt Street towards North Quay. Only the 2nd and 3rd bedroom of this apartment are likely to have outlook towards the proposal building, but as is typical for side facing units, it would not be unreasonable for windows to be smaller or even narrow and vertical on an apartment’s secondary side boundary facing frontage to foster privacy… iv. Apartment type B, C, D and E will enjoy excellent access to northeasterly breeze. Apartment type A will enjoy excellent access to southeasterly breeze.”

[200]The comprehensive analysis undertaken by Dr Robinson comfortably demonstrates that the separation provided between the proposed development and the Yi Shiu concept plan is sufficient to achieve compliance with overall outcome 3i.

[201]The appellants’ experts, namely Mr Buckley, Mr Olsson and Mr Richards, did not regard the setback of the proposed tower to be sufficient for a number of reasons. I will deal with Mr Buckley’s views directly in relation to performance outcome PO1 of the SHNPC. This is because the views he expressed about setbacks go hand-in-hand with the height and scale of the development and its visual impression. Mr Buckley described the resulting visual impression as, inter alia, cramped. Mr Olsson was of the view that the setback of the proposal to the western boundary would provide insufficient separation to secure an appropriate privacy outcome for future dwellings on the Yi Shiu land. He was also concerned that the proposed development would, by reason of its height, impede views to the CBD and beyond. This point assumed easterly views would be obscured by the proposed development above the 10 storey acceptable solution height for the site. This metric is derived from acceptable outcome AO1 of the SHNPC. Mr Richards’ evidence was less clear. After his oral evidence, I understood him to be concerned about a lack of separation manifesting in privacy and overlooking impacts, and a reduction in access to daylight.

[202]I am satisfied the photomontages, in particular viewpoints B and I1 and I2, do not make good that the setback provided by the proposed development to the common boundary with the Yi Shiu land is insufficient in so far as it is said to result in cramping and over-scaling. Further, Dr Robinson’s thorough examination also establishes that the concerns raised in relation to a reduction in access to sunlight/daylight, privacy and breezes does not warrant refusal here. As to a loss of views, the points raised by Mr Olsson are legitimate, but the evidence did not give sufficient weight, in my view, to two factors, namely: (1) the design of the Yi Shiu concept is likely to be refined; and (2) the refined design is likely to maximise views to the north, north-east, south and east. These views are expansive and will be available irrespective of the separation distance achieved at the common boundary with the site.

[203]I am satisfied the evidence establishes that the difference between the setback provided at the western boundary and the applicable acceptable outcome in AO3 of the SHNPC (being 1.5 metres) will not, as Dr McGowan said, make a great deal of difference.

[204]While the proposed development does not, at first blush, have the appearance of a slender tower, I am satisfied the planning purpose articulated for overall outcome 3i is met. Compliance is therefore demonstrated. Alternatively, any non-compliance is technical because it does not sound in an adverse planning consequence of the kind that would fall short of the planning purpose sought to be achieved by overall outcome 3i. Overall outcome 6b

[205]Overall outcome 6b is one of five specific provisions applying to the Boundary Street heart precinct. It requires development to be of a height and scale subordinate to the City Centre expansion precinct. The provision is extracted at

[98]. As I have already observed, it has in mind a particular relationship between built form in different precincts; development in the Boundary Street heart precinct is to be subordinate (in its three dimensions) to development in the City Centre expansion precinct: see

[100].

[206]The visual relationship between the height and scale of the proposed development and development in the City Centre expansion precinct can be assessed having regard to existing and approved development in that precinct.

[207]The City Centre expansion precinct is an area of land bounded by the southern side of Leichhardt Street, the western side of Upper Edward Street, Wickham Terrace and the eastern side of Wharf Street. Within this area, topography is undulating. In general terms, land falls from Wickham Terrace to Astor Terrace then rises sharply up to Leichhardt Street. Visual aids demonstrate that the undulating topography of the precinct has resulted in a fragmented skyline, which is most evident comparing the height of existing built form on Leichhardt Street with Astor Terrace.

[208]The existing built form character of the City Centre expansion precinct is mixed with built form varying in height (ranging from 3 to 27 storeys), scale, bulk, age and style. The observable differences in built form are, in many cases, exaggerated by undulating topography and the underlying subdivision pattern.

[209]The mix of built form in the City Centre expansion precinct includes development that is greater in height and scale than the proposed development. Two examples can be identified on Wharf Street. One building is a 27 storey high rise office tower at 152 Wharf Street. The material change of use approval for this building was granted by the Court: Peach v Brisbane City Council & Anor

[2020] QPELR 54. The three dimensions of this development, taken in combination, are appreciably greater in comparison to the proposed development. The second example is under construction on Wharf Street. When completed, it will be in the order of 30 storeys. It is taller than the proposed development.

[210]The City Centre expansion precinct will continue to include built form that is greater in height and scale than the proposed development. This is sufficiently clear having regard to: (1) extant development approvals granted for land in the City Centre expansion precinct; and (2) provisions of the SHNPC, which promote and anticipate substantial redevelopment and amalgamation of land in the City Centre expansion precinct.

[211]As to item (1), in 2023, Council granted an approval for land on Leichhardt Street in the City Centre expansion precinct. I have discussed the approval at

[22]. The approval is for land at 157 Leichhardt Street and authorises a mixed used development in a podium and tower form in the order of 23 storeys in height. The approved built form is ‘ghosted’ in the photomontages, allowing for a direct comparison with the height and scale of the proposed development. I am satisfied a comparative exercise of this kind demonstrates the proposed development would be subordinate to the approved built form, which is located in the City Centre expansion precinct. This is consistent with what is intended by overall outcome 6b.

[212]Item (2), in my view, is made good having regard to, inter alia: (a) overall outcome 4b of the SHNPC, which expressly promotes the development of the City Centre expansion precinct ‘as a strong economic centre’ accommodating ‘the highest densities and most diverse range of uses at a scale that is subordinate to the CBD’; (b) acceptable outcome AO1 and Table 7.2.19.5.3.B – Maximum building height and maximum tower site cover of the SHNPC, which incentivises the amalgamation of land and anticipates built form ranging from 3 to 30 storeys in the City Centre expansion precinct; and (c) precinct specific provisions of the SHNPC, such as overall outcomes 5a and 6b, that expressly constrain development height and scale where located outside of the City Centre expansion precinct.

[213]I am satisfied the matters traversed in

[207] to

[212] support a conclusion that the height and scale of the proposed development will be subordinate to development in the City Centre expansion precinct. That this conclusion is the correct one is confirmed in light of the following four matters, taken in combination, which apply to the Boundary Street heart precinct.

[214]First, a 15 storey high rise building, with setbacks the same as a 10 storey building, is contemplated in the Boundary Street heart precinct on a site that is 1,800m 2 or greater: Tables 7.2.19.5.3.B and D.

[215]Second, as was correctly submitted by Mr Job KC and Ms Rodgers, the site will be flanked with development of a height and scale that is anticipated in the Boundary Street heart precinct. This is evident from the implementation of an approval attaching to 25 Fortescue Street: see

[19] and

[20]. This development is ghosted in the photomontages for comparative purposes. The photomontages confirm the approved development: (1) is appreciably greater in height and scale in comparison to existing built form in the block identified at

[14]; (2) is comparable to the height of the proposed development; and (3) is appreciably greater in scale in comparison to the proposed development.

[216]Third, a 15 storey high rise building, with the same setbacks as a 10 storey building is contemplated on land adjoining the site to the west. This is demonstrated by the Yi Shiu concept. The so-called code compliant development depicted in those plans presents a significant façade to Leichhardt Street. It is comparable in height but greater in scale than the proposed development.

[217]Fourth, despite the differences in building height and scale anticipated for the different precincts of the neighbourhood plan area, the SHNPC (see

[95] and

[96]): (a) does not require development to achieve a gradation in building height or scale within precincts (i.e. gradation of building height and scale from the centre of a precinct down to its edge); and (b) does not require development to achieve a gradation in building height or scale at the edge of a precinct where it shares a common boundary with the City Centre expansion precinct.

[218]Subparagraphs (a) and (b) suggest, in my view, that built form lining either side of a common precinct boundary in the SHNPC will not necessarily read as hard contrasting elements. Differences in the height and scale of built form between the precincts will likely be more subtle. This will be the case on Leichhardt Street given: (1) the undulating topography, north and south of the road, which impacts on the perceived heights of buildings and fragments the skyline; (2) from some vantage points, such as the photomontages for viewpoint D, built form on either side of the road blends together as a clump – this is due to the meandering alignment of the road along a ridgeline; and (3) there is an overlap in terms of building heights anticipated in the different precincts (see Table 7.2.19.5.3.B of the SHNPC) – as the Yi Shiu concept plan demonstrates, development of significant height and scale is not limited to the City Centre expansion precinct.

[219]Alleged non-compliance with overall outcome 6b of the SHNPC was pressed as an important part of the appellants’ refusal case. In summary terms, it was submitted there was non-compliance with overall outcome 6b because the proposed development: (a) will be visually prominent, and read as part of the City Centre expansion precinct; (b) will be aligned with, rather than subordinate to, development in the City Centre expansion precinct; (c) will appreciably ‘bleed’ into a different ‘in-between’ category of built form (in terms of height and scale) given: (i) the closest part of the City Centre expansion precinct is unlikely to be developed to 25 or 30 storeys in the foreseeable future given the size, state and nature of existing development; (ii) it will be the tallest development in the Boundary street heart precinct (in storeys); and (iii) it would compete with the Spring Hill District centre along Boundary Street in circumstances where the District centre is to ‘accommodate slightly higher densities than surrounding neighbourhoods’: Ex.63, para 12.

[220]With respect to (a), I accept the proposed development will be visually prominent given the location and elevation of the site. This is not, however, unacceptable. For reasons already given, I am satisfied the height, bulk and scale of the proposed development will be appropriate in its context, both existing and planned. This is made good, in my view, primarily by the photomontages.

[221]I do not accept subparagraph (b), and (a) to the extent it overlaps. The proposed development will, for reasons given above, be subordinate to the height and scale of development in the City Centre expansion precinct.

[222]As to subparagraph (c), the substance of the submission advances the proposition that the proposed development ‘bleeds’ into a different category by applying or borrowing development parameters applicable to the City Centre expansion precinct to the site. I do not accept this is correct for reasons already given.
